### 2.7 Why These Methods Matter in Biomedical Engineering

- **Gauss Elimination** is the numerical engine behind **ECG/EEG source localization** (solving the lead-field equations).
- **Gauss-Seidel** dominates **diffusion-based MRI image reconstruction** where the system matrix is **sparse and huge** ($10^6 \times 10^6$).
- **LU Decomposition** is the backbone of **real-time patient monitoring** because $L$ and $U$ are computed once and reused for every new $\vec{b}$ (e.g., new measurement vector).
- **Cramer's Rule** is used symbolically in **small-scale biomechanical joint-force analysis** (e.g., 2D/3D static equilibrium of a limb).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Gauss Elimination — The Multiplier Formula

**Starting Point:** Row $i$ of the system at step $k$ is:

$$a_{i1}x_1 + a_{i2}x_2 + \dots + a_{in}x_n = b_i, \quad i = k, k+1, \dots, n$$

**Goal:** Eliminate $x_k$ from row $i$ ($i > k$).

**Step 1 — Define the multiplier.** The multiplier $m_{ik}$ is the ratio that makes the coefficient of $x_k$ vanish:

$$m_{ik} = \frac{a_{ik}^{(k)}}{a_{kk}^{(k)}}$$

The notation $a_{ik}^{(k)}$ means the entry $a_{ik}$ *after* $k-1$ elimination steps.

**Step 2 — Apply the elementary row operation.** Subtract $m_{ik}$ times row $k$ from row $i$:

$$R_i \leftarrow R_i - m_{ik} R_k$$

**Step 3 — Verify the elimination.** The new coefficient of $x_k$ in row $i$ is:

$$a_{ik}^{(k+1)} = a_{ik}^{(k)} - m_{ik} \cdot a_{kk}^{(k)} = a_{ik}^{(k)} - \frac{a_{ik}^{(k)}}{a_{kk}^{(k)}} \cdot a_{kk}^{(k)} = 0$$

**Step 4 — General update rule for $j > k$:**

$$a_{ij}^{(k+1)} = a_{ij}^{(k)} - m_{ik} a_{kj}^{(k)} = a_{ij}^{(k)} - \frac{a_{ik}^{(k)}}{a_{kk}^{(k)}} a_{kj}^{(k)}$$

**Step 5 — Update rule for RHS:**

$$b_i^{(k+1)} = b_i^{(k)} - m_{ik} b_k^{(k)} = b_i^{(k)} - \frac{a_{ik}^{(k)}}{a_{kk}^{(k)}} b_k^{(k)}$$

**Step 6 — Back substitution (after $n-1$ elimination steps):**

$$x_n = \frac{b_n^{(n-1)}}{a_{nn}^{(n-1)}}$$

$$x_i = \frac{1}{a_{ii}^{(i-1)}}\left(b_i^{(i-1)} - \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} a_{ij}^{(i-1)} x_j\right), \quad i = n-1, n-2, \dots, 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** Elementary row operations preserve the solution set of the linear system. We are merely re-expressing the same equations in a more convenient (triangular) form.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Gauss Elimination on a 3×3 Biomedical System

**Problem:** A pharmacokinetic 3-compartment model yields:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & -1 \\
3 & 3 & 9 \\
3 & 1 & -5 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 8 \\ 24 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Find the drug concentrations $x_1, x_2, x_3$ in compartments 1, 2, 3.

**Augmented Matrix:**

$$
\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c} 2 & 1 & -1 & 8 \\ 3 & 3 & 9 & 24 \\ 3 & 1 & -5 & 2 \end{array}\right]
$$

**Step 1 — Eliminate $x_1$ from rows 2 and 3 using row 1.**

Multiplier for row 2:

$$m_{21} = \frac{a_{21}}{a_{11}} = \frac{3}{2}$$

Multiplier for row 3:

$$m_{31} = \frac{a_{31}}{a_{11}} = \frac{3}{2}$$

Apply $R_2 \leftarrow R_2 - \frac{3}{2}R_1$:

$$R_2 = [3, 3, 9, 24] - \frac{3}{2}[2, 1, -1, 8] = [0, \frac{3}{2}, \frac{21}{2}, 12]$$

Apply $R_3 \leftarrow R_3 - \frac{3}{2}R_1$:

$$R_3 = [3, 1, -5, 2] - \frac{3}{2}[2, 1, -1, 8] = [0, -\frac{1}{2}, -\frac{7}{2}, -10]$$

Updated matrix:

$$
\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c} 2 & 1 & -1 & 8 \\ 0 & 1.5 & 10.5 & 12 \\ 0 & -0.5 & -3.5 & -10 \end{array}\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Eliminate $x_2$ from row 3 using row 2.**

Multiplier:

$$m_{32} = \frac{-0.5}{1.5} = -\frac{1}{3}$$

Apply $R_3 \leftarrow R_3 - (-\frac{1}{3})R_2 = R_3 + \frac{1}{3}R_2$:

$$R_3 = [0, -0.5, -3.5, -10] + \frac{1}{3}[0, 1.5, 10.5, 12]$$
$$= [0, 0, -3.5 + 3.5, -10 + 4] = [0, 0, 0, -6]$$

**Step 3 — Diagnose.** The system has a row of zeros on the left with $-6$ on the right. **The system is inconsistent** — the pharmacokinetic model as stated has no physically valid solution (likely a data error in compartment 3).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always check for $\det(A) \neq 0$ before applying elimination. Here, $\det(A) = 0$ — confirmed by the appearance of a zero row. This is a favorite KTU trick question.

### 3.3 Worked Example — Gauss-Seidel Iteration

**Problem:** Solve the system

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 5 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & 6 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 6 \\ 8 \end{bmatrix}
$$

starting with $\vec{x}^{(0)} = [0, 0, 0]^{T}$, performing 3 iterations.

**Step 0 — Check Diagonal Dominance:**

Row 1: $\vert 4 \vert \geq \vert 1 \vert + \vert 2 \vert = 3$ ✓ (strict)
Row 2: $\vert 5 \vert \geq \vert 1 \vert + \vert 1 \vert = 2$ ✓ (strict)
Row 3: $\vert 6 \vert \geq \vert 2 \vert + \vert 1 \vert = 3$ ✓ (strict)

Strict diagonal dominance holds → **convergence is guaranteed**.

**Step 1 — Derive the iteration formulas:**

$$x_1^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{4}\left(4 - x_2^{(k)} - 2x_3^{(k)}\right)$$

$$x_2^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{5}\left(6 - x_1^{(k+1)} - x_3^{(k)}\right)$$

$$x_3^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{6}\left(8 - 2x_1^{(k+1)} - x_2^{(k+1)}\right)$$

**Iteration 1 ($k = 0$):**

$$x_1^{(1)} = \frac{1}{4}(4 - 0 - 0) = 1.000000$$

$$x_2^{(1)} = \frac{1}{5}(6 - 1.000000 - 0) = \frac{5}{5} = 1.000000$$

$$x_3^{(1)} = \frac{1}{6}(8 - 2(1.000000) - 1.000000) = \frac{5}{6} = 0.833333$$

$$\vec{x}^{(1)} = [1.000000, \; 1.000000, \; 0.833333]^{T}$$

**Iteration 2 ($k = 1$):**

$$x_1^{(2)} = \frac{1}{4}(4 - 1.000000 - 2(0.833333)) = \frac{1}{4}(1.333334) = 0.333333$$

$$x_2^{(2)} = \frac{1}{5}(6 - 0.333333 - 0.833333) = \frac{4.833334}{5} = 0.966667$$

$$x_3^{(2)} = \frac{1}{6}(8 - 2(0.333333) - 0.966667) = \frac{6.366667}{6} = 1.061111$$

$$\vec{x}^{(2)} = [0.333333, \; 0.966667, \; 1.061111]^{T}$$

**Iteration 3 ($k = 2$):**

$$x_1^{(3)} = \frac{1}{4}(4 - 0.966667 - 2(1.061111)) = \frac{0.911111}{4} = 0.227778$$

$$x_2^{(3)} = \frac{1}{5}(6 - 0.227778 - 1.061111) = \frac{4.711111}{5} = 0.942222$$

$$x_3^{(3)} = \frac{1}{6}(8 - 2(0.227778) - 0.942222) = \frac{6.602222}{6} = 1.100370$$

$$\vec{x}^{(3)} = [0.227778, \; 0.942222, \; 1.100370]^{T}$$

**Verification of Convergence:** The true solution (via direct methods) is $\vec{x} = [0.222222, \; 0.944444, \; 1.111111]^{T} = [\frac{2}{9}, \frac{17}{18}, \frac{10}{9}]^{T}$. Errors are shrinking rapidly:

- $\|\vec{x}^{(1)} - \vec{x}\| \approx 1.19$
- $\|\vec{x}^{(2)} - \vec{x}\| \approx 0.11$
- $\|\vec{x}^{(3)} - \vec{x}\| \approx 0.02$

Convergence is **monotonic and geometric**.

### 3.4 Derivation of the Gauss-Seidel Iteration Matrix

**Starting from the matrix splitting** $A = D - L - U$ (diagonal, strictly lower, strictly upper parts):

$$(D - L - U)\vec{x} = \vec{b}$$
$$(D - L)\vec{x}^{(k+1)} = U\vec{x}^{(k)} + \vec{b}$$
$$\vec{x}^{(k+1)} = (D - L)^{-1}U \vec{x}^{(k)} + (D - L)^{-1}\vec{b}$$

Therefore the **Gauss-Seidel iteration matrix** is:

$$T_{GS} = (D - L)^{-1}U$$

and the **convergence criterion** is:

$$\rho(T_{GS}) = \max_{\lambda \in \sigma(T_{GS})} \vert \lambda \vert < 1$$

Similarly, the **Jacobi iteration matrix** is:

$$T_J = D^{-1}(L + U)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Relationship:** Since $\rho(T_{GS}) = \rho(T_J)^2$ in many practical symmetric positive-definite cases, Gauss-Seidel typically converges **roughly twice as fast** as Jacobi.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Linear System Solution Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Biomedical Problem Statement] --> B[Identify Unknowns x1 x2 ... xn]
    B --> C[Apply Governing Laws Mass Balance Kirchhoff Fick]
    C --> D[Linearize Each Equation]
    D --> E[Assemble Coefficient Matrix A and RHS Vector b]
    E --> F{System Size and Structure}
    F -->|n small and dense| G[Direct Methods]
    F -->|n large and sparse| H[Iterative Methods]
    G --> G1[Cramer Rule n le 3]
    G --> G2[Gauss Elimination with Pivoting]
    G --> G3[Gauss Jordan Elimination]
    G --> G4[LU Decomposition]
    H --> H1[Jacobi Iteration]
    H --> H2[Gauss Seidel Iteration]
    H --> H3[SOR with Relaxation Factor omega]
    G2 --> I1[Forward Elimination then Back Substitution]
    G3 --> I2[Forward and Backward Elimination to RREF]
    H1 --> J1[Check Diagonal Dominance]
    H2 --> J1
    H1 --> K1[Initialize x0 and Iterate xk+1 equals T xk plus c]
    H2 --> K1
    K1 --> L{Convergence rho of T less than 1}
    L -->|Yes| M[Solution Vector x]
    L -->|No| N[Apply SOR or Preconditioning]
    I1 --> M
    I2 --> M
    N --> M
    M --> O[Biomedical Interpretation Concentrations Currents Displacements]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Gauss-Seidel Iteration Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    InA[Coefficient Matrix A n x n] --> Splitter[Matrix Splitter A equals D minus L minus U]
    Splitter --> Dblock[Diagonal Block D]
    Splitter --> Lblock[Lower Block L]
    Splitter --> Ublock[Upper Block U]
    Bvec[RHS Vector b] --> Compute
    Xinit[Initial Guess x superscript 0] --> Compute
    Dblock --> Compute[Update Module x k+1 equals D minus L inverse times U xk plus b]
    Lblock --> Compute
    Ublock --> Compute
    Compute --> Check{Convergence Achieved}
    Check -->|No| Compute
    Check -->|Yes| OutX[Final Solution x]
    OutX --> Bio[Biomedical Output e.g. Drug Concentrations]
```

### 4.3 Decision Flowchart: Choosing Between Direct and Iterative

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Given Linear System A x equals b] --> Q1{Is n small less than 100?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Need multiple RHS vectors?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Is A sparse?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| P1[Use LU Decomposition compute L and U once]
    Q2 -->|No| P2[Use Gauss Elimination]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Is A symmetric positive definite?}
    Q3 -->|No| P3[Use Gauss Elimination with full pivoting]
    Q4 -->|Yes| P4[Use Conjugate Gradient or Cholesky]
    Q4 -->|No| Q5{Is A diagonally dominant?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| P5[Use Gauss Seidel guaranteed convergence]
    Q5 -->|No| P6[Use SOR with omega search or BiCGSTAB]
```

### 4.4 Pipeline: From Biomedical Data to Linear System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Patient ECG Signals 12 leads] --> P2[Build Lead Field Matrix A]
    P2 --> P3[Formulate A x equals b]
    P3 --> P4[Precondition: Scale Rows]
    P4 --> P5[Apply Gauss Elimination or Gauss Seidel]
    P5 --> P6[Cardiac Source Localization x]
    P6 --> P7[Clinical Diagnosis and Therapy Planning]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a system of linear algebraic equations. What is the necessary and sufficient condition for a unique solution?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A system of linear algebraic equations is a finite collection of $n$ linear equations in $n$ unknowns that can be written in matrix form as $A\vec{x} = \vec{b}$, where $A$ is the $n \times n$ coefficient matrix, $\vec{x}$ is the unknown vector, and $\vec{b}$ is the RHS vector. **(1 mark)**
The necessary and sufficient condition for a unique solution is that the coefficient matrix $A$ must be **non-singular**, i.e., $\det(A) \neq 0$. **(1 mark)**
Equivalently, the rows (and columns) of $A$ must be linearly independent, and $A$ must have a non-zero determinant. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is partial pivoting in Gauss Elimination? Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Partial pivoting is a row-swap strategy applied before each elimination step in Gauss Elimination. **(1 mark)**
At the start of step $k$, we locate the row $p \in \{k, k+1, \dots, n\}$ with the largest absolute value $\vert a_{pk}^{(k)} \vert$ in column $k$, and swap row $p$ with row $k$. **(1 mark)**
It is necessary to (i) prevent division by zero when the pivot $a_{kk}^{(k)} = 0$, and (ii) reduce **round-off errors** that accumulate when dividing by very small pivots, thereby improving numerical stability. **(1 mark)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3 (A).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, Modified]`

**(a)** Formulate the system of linear algebraic equations that represents the following 3-compartment pharmacokinetic model. Compartment 1 (plasma) has a clearance rate of 2, transfers 1 unit to compartment 2 (tissue) and 1 unit to compartment 3 (deep tissue). Compartment 2 clears 3 units, transfers 1 unit back to compartment 1. Compartment 3 clears 2 units and receives 1 from compartment 1. The system input (drug infusion) into compartment 1 is 10 units/hour, into compartment 2 is 0, into compartment 3 is 0. At steady state, the net rate of change in each compartment is zero. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Solve the formulated system using **Gauss Elimination with partial pivoting** to find the steady-state drug concentrations $x_1, x_2, x_3$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Formulation:**

Let $x_1, x_2, x_3$ be the steady-state drug concentrations in compartments 1, 2, 3 respectively.

Applying mass balance at steady state (rate in = rate out):

**Compartment 1:** Input + Transfer from 2 = Outflow (clearance + transfer out)
$$0 + 1 \cdot x_2 = 2 x_1 + 1 \cdot x_1 + 1 \cdot x_1$$
$$-4x_1 + x_2 + 0 \cdot x_3 + 10 = 0$$
$$4x_1 - x_2 = 10 \quad \text{...(Eq. 1)}$$

**Compartment 2:** Input + Transfer from 1 = Outflow
$$0 + 1 \cdot x_1 = 3 x_2 + 1 \cdot x_2$$
$$-x_1 + 4x_2 = 0 \quad \text{...(Eq. 2)}$$

**Compartment 3:** Input + Transfer from 1 = Outflow
$$0 + 1 \cdot x_1 = 2 x_3$$
$$-x_1 + 2x_3 = 0 \quad \text{...(Eq. 3)}$$

**Matrix Form:** (Rearranging $A\vec{x} = \vec{b}$)

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 4 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 4 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> (3 marks for the equations, 2 marks for the matrix form, 2 marks for the verbal explanation of mass balance at steady state.)

---

**Part (b) — Solution by Gauss Elimination with Partial Pivoting:**

**Augmented Matrix:**

$$
\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c} 4 & -1 & 0 & 10 \\ -1 & 4 & 0 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 2 & 0 \end{array}\right]
$$

**Step 1 — Pivoting for column 1.** The largest $\vert a_{i1} \vert$ is $\vert 4 \vert$ in row 1. Row 1 is already the pivot row. No swap needed.

**Step 2 — Eliminate $x_1$ from rows 2 and 3.**

$$m_{21} = \frac{-1}{4} = -0.25$$
$$m_{31} = \frac{-1}{4} = -0.25$$

Apply $R_2 \leftarrow R_2 - m_{21}R_1 = R_2 + 0.25 R_1$:

$$R_2 = [-1, 4, 0, 0] + 0.25[4, -1, 0, 10]$$
$$= [-1 + 1, \; 4 - 0.25, \; 0, \; 0 + 2.5] = [0, \; 3.75, \; 0, \; 2.5]$$

Apply $R_3 \leftarrow R_3 - m_{31}R_1 = R_3 + 0.25 R_1$:

$$R_3 = [-1, 0, 2, 0] + 0.25[4, -1, 0, 10]$$
$$= [-1 + 1, \; -0.25, \; 2, \; 2.5] = [0, \; -0.25, \; 2, \; 2.5]$$

Updated matrix:

$$
\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c} 4 & -1 & 0 & 10 \\ 0 & 3.75 & 0 & 2.5 \\ 0 & -0.25 & 2 & 2.5 \end{array}\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Pivoting for column 2.** Largest $\vert a_{i2} \vert$ for $i \in \{2,3\}$ is $\vert 3.75 \vert$ in row 2. No swap needed.

**Step 4 — Eliminate $x_2$ from row 3.**

$$m_{32} = \frac{-0.25}{3.75} = -\frac{1}{15}$$

Apply $R_3 \leftarrow R_3 - m_{32} R_2 = R_3 + \frac{1}{15}R_2$:

$$R_3 = [0, -0.25, 2, 2.5] + \frac{1}{15}[0, 3.75, 0, 2.5]$$
$$= [0, \; -0.25 + 0.25, \; 2, \; 2.5 + 0.166667] = [0, \; 0, \; 2, \; 2.666667]$$

Updated matrix:

$$
\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c} 4 & -1 & 0 & 10 \\ 0 & 3.75 & 0 & 2.5 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 & 2.666667 \end{array}\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Back Substitution:**

$$x_3 = \frac{2.666667}{2} = 1.333333$$

$$x_2 = \frac{2.5 - 0 \cdot x_3}{3.75} = \frac{2.5}{3.75} = 0.666667$$

$$x_1 = \frac{10 - (-1)x_2 - 0 \cdot x_3}{4} = \frac{10 + 0.666667}{4} = \frac{10.666667}{4} = 2.666667$$

**Final Answer:** $x_1 = 2.6667$, $x_2 = 0.6667$, $x_3 = 1.3333$ (units of drug concentration).

> (1 mark pivoting, 2 marks multipliers, 2 marks elimination, 1 mark back substitution, 1 mark final answer with units.)

---

**Q3 (B).** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1, Modified]` **(Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Explain the **Gauss-Seidel iterative method** for solving $A\vec{x} = \vec{b}$. State the iteration formula and the sufficient condition for convergence. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the linear system
$$
\begin{bmatrix} 10 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 12 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 8 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 13 \\ 23 \\ 17 \end{bmatrix}
$$
starting with $\vec{x}^{(0)} = [0, 0, 0]^{T}$, perform **4 iterations** of the Gauss-Seidel method. Show all intermediate values to 4 decimal places. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Theory (7 marks):**

The Gauss-Seidel method is an **iterative technique** for solving $A\vec{x} = \vec{b}$ by refining an initial guess $\vec{x}^{(0)}$ through successive updates. **(1 mark)**

We split the coefficient matrix as $A = L_* + U$ where $L_* = D + L$ (lower-triangular part including the diagonal) and $U$ is the strictly upper-triangular part. The iteration formula is derived from $L_* \vec{x}^{(k+1)} = \vec{b} - U \vec{x}^{(k)}$, giving: **(2 marks)**

$$x_i^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{a_{ii}}\left(b_i - \sum_{j < i} a_{ij}x_j^{(k+1)} - \sum_{j > i} a_{ij}x_j^{(k)}\right)$$

The key feature is that as soon as $x_1^{(k+1)}, x_2^{(k+1)}, \dots$ are computed, they are used immediately in the same iteration. **(1 mark)**

**Sufficient condition for convergence (Diagonal Dominance Theorem):** If $A$ is **strictly diagonally dominant**, i.e.,

$$\vert a_{ii} \vert > \sum_{j \neq i} \vert a_{ij} \vert \quad \text{for all } i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

then the Gauss-Seidel method converges from any initial guess $\vec{x}^{(0)}$. **(2 marks)**

Additionally, the **spectral radius condition** $\rho(T_{GS}) < 1$ is a necessary and sufficient condition, where $T_{GS} = (D - L)^{-1}U$ is the iteration matrix. **(1 mark)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Check Diagonal Dominance:**
- Row 1: $\vert 10 \vert > \vert 1 \vert + \vert 2 \vert = 3$ ✓
- Row 2: $\vert 12 \vert > \vert 2 \vert + \vert 1 \vert = 3$ ✓
- Row 3: $\vert 8 \vert > \vert 1 \vert + \vert 1 \vert = 2$ ✓

**Convergence is guaranteed.** ✓

**Derive the iteration formulas:**

$$x_1^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{10}\left(13 - x_2^{(k)} - 2x_3^{(k)}\right)$$

$$x_2^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{12}\left(23 - 2x_1^{(k+1)} - x_3^{(k)}\right)$$

$$x_3^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{8}\left(17 - x_1^{(k+1)} - x_2^{(k+1)}\right)$$

**Iteration 1** ($x_1^{(0)} = x_2^{(0)} = x_3^{(0)} = 0$):

$$x_1^{(1)} = \frac{1}{10}(13 - 0 - 0) = 1.3000$$

$$x_2^{(1)} = \frac{1}{12}(23 - 2(1.3000) - 0) = \frac{20.4000}{12} = 1.7000$$

$$x_3^{(1)} = \frac{1}{8}(17 - 1.3000 - 1.7000) = \frac{14.0000}{8} = 1.7500$$

$$\vec{x}^{(1)} = [1.3000, \; 1.7000, \; 1.7500]^{T}$$

**Iteration 2:**

$$x_1^{(2)} = \frac{1}{10}(13 - 1.7000 - 2(1.7500)) = \frac{7.8000}{10} = 0.7800$$

$$x_2^{(2)} = \frac{1}{12}(23 - 2(0.7800) - 1.7500) = \frac{19.6900}{12} = 1.6408$$

$$x_3^{(2)} = \frac{1}{8}(17 - 0.7800 - 1.6408) = \frac{14.5792}{8} = 1.8224$$

$$\vec{x}^{(2)} = [0.7800, \; 1.6408, \; 1.8224]^{T}$$

**Iteration 3:**

$$x_1^{(3)} = \frac{1}{10}(13 - 1.6408 - 2(1.8224)) = \frac{7.7144}{10} = 0.7714$$

$$x_2^{(3)} = \frac{1}{12}(23 - 2(0.7714) - 1.8224) = \frac{19.6348}{12} = 1.6362$$

$$x_3^{(3)} = \frac{1}{8}(17 - 0.7714 - 1.6362) = \frac{14.5924}{8} = 1.8241$$

$$\vec{x}^{(3)} = [0.7714, \; 1.6362, \; 1.8241]^{T}$$

**Iteration 4:**

$$x_1^{(4)} = \frac{1}{10}(13 - 1.6362 - 2(1.8241)) = \frac{7.7156}{10} = 0.7716$$

$$x_2^{(4)} = \frac{1}{12}(23 - 2(0.7716) - 1.8241) = \frac{19.6327}{12} = 1.6361$$

$$x_3^{(4)} = \frac{1}{8}(17 - 0.7716 - 1.6361) = \frac{14.5923}{8} = 1.8240$$

$$\vec{x}^{(4)} = [0.7716, \; 1.6361, \; 1.8240]^{T}$$

**True Solution (for comparison):** $\vec{x} = [0.7716, \; 1.6361, \; 1.8240]^{T}$ — the iteration has converged to 4 decimal places in just 4 iterations.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in $m_{ik}$:** Many students compute $m_{ik} = a_{kk}/a_{ik}$ instead of $a_{ik}/a_{kk}$. The multiplier is **always row to be eliminated over pivot row**.
> 2. **Forgetting to update RHS:** The right-hand side vector $\vec{b}$ must be modified by the *same* row operation as $A$ — this is a 1-mark loss per occurrence.
> 3. **Back-substitution order error:** Start from $x_n$ and work *upward* (decreasing index), not downward.
> 4. **Gauss-Seidel vs Jacobi confusion:** In Gauss-Seidel, $x_1^{(k+1)}$ uses the *new* value already computed in this iteration, NOT the old $x_1^{(k)}$. Using only old values turns the formula into Jacobi — full 7-mark loss.
> 5. **Skipping pivoting in elimination:** Even if the system is solvable, KTU expects pivoting to be mentioned for full marks.
> 6. **No convergence check:** Always state the diagonal dominance check before starting Gauss-Seidel iteration.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Linear algebraic equation system** = canonical form $A\vec{x} = \vec{b}$ where $A$ is $n \times n$.
- [x] **Unique solution exists** iff $\det(A) \neq 0$ (non-singular $A$).
- [x] **Formulation pipeline** = Identify unknowns → Apply governing laws → Linearize → Assemble matrix.
- [x] **Cramer's Rule** is $\mathcal{O}(n!)$ — only for $n \leq 3$ symbolically.
- [x] **Gauss Elimination** has two phases: Forward Elimination (multiply $m_{ik} = a_{ik}/a_{kk}$, subtract) and Back Substitution.
- [x] **Partial pivoting** = swap the row with largest $\vert a_{ik} \vert$ into the pivot position before each elimination step.
- [x] **Gauss-Jordan** = reduce to RREF (diagonal = 1, off-diagonal = 0); no back-substitution needed.
- [x] **LU Decomposition** factors $A = LU$; same cost as Gauss but reuses factors for many RHS vectors.
- [x] **Jacobi iteration** uses only *old* iteration values: $x_i^{(k+1)} = (b_i - \sum_{j \neq i} a_{ij}x_j^{(k)})/a_{ii}$.
- [x] **Gauss-Seidel** uses *most recent* values immediately: $x_i^{(k+1)} = (b_i - \sum_{j<i} a_{ij}x_j^{(k+1)} - \sum_{j>i} a_{ij}x_j^{(k)})/a_{ii}$.
- [x] **Diagonal Dominance Theorem:** $\vert a_{ii} \vert > \sum_{j \neq i} \vert a_{ij} \vert$ for all $i$ guarantees convergence of Jacobi and Gauss-Seidel.
- [x] **Spectral radius condition:** $\rho(T) < 1$ is the necessary and sufficient convergence criterion.
- [x] **SOR parameter** $\omega \in (0, 2)$ — $\omega = 1$ recovers Gauss-Seidel; $\omega > 1$ is over-relaxation (faster); $\omega < 1$ is under-relaxation.
- [x] **Biomedical applications:** ECG lead equations, MRI reconstruction, pharmacokinetic compartmental models, biomechanical FEM, CT image reconstruction.
- [x] **Memory and time complexity of Gauss Elimination:** $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ memory, $\mathcal{O}(n^3/3)$ flops.
- [x] **Iterative methods** are preferred for **sparse** systems where direct methods waste time on zeros.
- [x] **Cramer's rule is NEVER used in production biomedical software** — it is for **pedagogical** understanding only.
- [x] **Always check for ill-conditioning** by computing the condition number $\kappa(A) = \vert\vert A \vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert A^{-1} \vert\vert$ — large $\kappa$ ⇒ sensitive to data errors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gauss Elimination Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT634 - COMPUTATIONAL METHODS IN BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Gauss Elimination Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Compliant):**  
> The **Gauss Elimination Method** is a direct (non-iterative) numerical algorithm used to determine the solution vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T$ of a system of $n$ linear algebraic equations expressed in matrix form as $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$, where $A$ is an $n \times n$ non-singular coefficient matrix and $\mathbf{b}$ is the constant (right-hand-side) vector. The algorithm proceeds in two distinct phases: **(i) Forward Elimination**, in which the coefficient matrix is reduced to an upper triangular form using a finite sequence of elementary row operations, and **(ii) Back Substitution**, in which the unknowns are recovered sequentially starting from the last equation.

The method transforms the original system $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ into an equivalent upper triangular system $U\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{c}$ by applying a sequence of elementary row operations, while preserving the solution set at every step.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]  
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Pyramid of Clues":**  
> Imagine you are a detective with three suspects, and you have three clues. Clue 1 talks about all three suspects. Clue 2 talks about all three. Clue 3 also mentions all three. Solving them all at once is overwhelming. The **Gauss Elimination Method** is like a strategy where you first eliminate one suspect (variable) from all but one clue — say, you remove suspect 3 from clues 2 and 3. Then you remove suspect 2 from clue 3. Now you have a "pyramid" of clues where the last clue talks only about suspect 1 (easy to solve!), the second-to-last talks about suspects 1 and 2 (use clue 1 to find suspect 1, then clue 2 to find suspect 2), and so on — solving from the bottom up.  
>  
> This is precisely **Forward Elimination** (building the pyramid) followed by **Back Substitution** (climbing the pyramid from bottom to top).

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Computational Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(n^3 / 3)$ arithmetic operations (flops) for forward elimination, plus $\mathcal{O}(n^2 / 2)$ for back substitution.
- **Total Operations:** Approximately $n^3 / 3 + n^2$ multiplications/divisions.
- **Condition Number Threshold (Rule of Thumb):** $A$ is considered numerically well-conditioned if $\text{cond}(A) < 10^k$, where $k$ is the number of significant decimal digits retained.
- **Pivoting Strategy:** Mandatory in real-world computation; recommended when $\vert a_{kk} \vert < \sum_{i \neq k} \vert a_{ki} \vert$.

### 1.4 Biomedical Engineering Context

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Why Biomedical Engineers Study Gauss Elimination:**  
> 1. **ECG/EEG Lead-Field Analysis:** Solving linear systems arising from volume-conductor models.  
> 2. **CT/MRI Image Reconstruction:** Solving the discrete Radon transform (system of linear equations for pixel intensities).  
> 3. **Compartmental Pharmacokinetics:** Modeling drug concentration in multiple body compartments.  
> 4. **Biomechanical Static Analysis:** Force/stress equilibrium in musculoskeletal models.  
> 5. **Neural Network Forward Pass:** Single-layer perceptron computations reduce to $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Algorithmic Logic Steps

The Gauss Elimination Method is decomposed into the following structured steps:

#### **Phase I — Forward Elimination**

1. **Augmented Matrix Formation:** Combine $A$ and $\mathbf{b}$ to form the augmented matrix $[A \vert \mathbf{b}]$ of dimension $n \times (n+1)$.

2. **Pivot Selection:** Identify the diagonal element $a_{kk}$ (called the *pivot*) at the $k$-th elimination step.

3. **Zero Out Sub-diagonal Elements:** For each row $i > k$, compute the **multiplier** $m_{ik}$:

$$m_{ik} = \frac{a_{ik}}{a_{kk}}$$

4. **Row Operation:** Replace row $i$ by $R_i \leftarrow R_i - m_{ik} \cdot R_k$ for all columns $j = k, k+1, \ldots, n+1$:

$$a_{ij}^{\text{new}} = a_{ij} - m_{ik} \cdot a_{kj}$$

5. **Iterate:** Increment $k$ from $1$ to $n-1$ and repeat.

6. **Result:** An upper triangular system $U\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{c}$.

#### **Phase II — Back Substitution**

1. Compute the last variable:

$$x_n = \frac{c_n}{u_{nn}} = \frac{a_{n,n+1}}{a_{nn}}$$

2. For $i = n-1, n-2, \ldots, 1$, compute:

$$x_i = \frac{1}{a_{ii}} \left( a_{i,n+1} - \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} a_{ij} \cdot x_j \right)$$

3. **Result:** The complete solution vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T$.

### 2.2 Why Each Step Matters — The "Why" Behind the Math

- **Why multipliers?** They make the leading element below the pivot exactly **zero**, which decouples the system progressively.
- **Why the pivot must be non-zero?** If $a_{kk} = 0$, the row operation is undefined (division by zero). This is why **pivoting** is essential.
- **Why back substitution from the bottom?** The upper triangular form means the last equation involves only $x_n$, the second-to-last involves $x_{n-1}$ and $x_n$ (with $x_n$ already known), and so on.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Component** | **Formula / Expression** | **Purpose / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| General linear system | $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ | $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| Augmented matrix | $[A \vert \mathbf{b}]$ | Dimension $n \times (n+1)$ |
| Multiplier | $m_{ik} = a_{ik} / a_{kk}$ | Computed at step $k$ for row $i > k$ |
| Row update | $a_{ij}^{\text{new}} = a_{ij} - m_{ik} \cdot a_{kj}$ | Applied for $j = k, \ldots, n+1$ |
| Last variable (back sub.) | $x_n = a_{n,n+1} / a_{nn}$ | Solved first in back substitution |
| General back-sub. formula | $x_i = \left( a_{i,n+1} - \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} a_{ij} x_j \right) / a_{ii}$ | For $i = n-1, n-2, \ldots, 1$ |
| Total operations | $\approx n^3/3 + n^2$ flops | Dominant cost is forward elimination |
| Pivoting condition | $\vert a_{kk} \vert \geq \sum_{i \neq k} \vert a_{ki} \vert$ | Sufficient condition to avoid zero pivot |
| Determinant (post-elimination) | $\det(A) = (-1)^p \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} a_{ii}$ | $p$ = number of row interchanges (pivots) |
| Matrix $L$ entry | $\ell_{ik} = m_{ik}$ | $L$ is unit lower triangular, $U$ is upper triangular |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **biomedical signal processing**, Gauss Elimination forms the computational backbone of:
- **Impedance tomography** for lung imaging.
- **Inverse problems** in magnetoencephalography (MEG).
- **Biomass balance equations** in pharmacokinetic compartmental models.
- **Finite element analysis (FEA)** of bone-prosthesis interfaces.

> [!NOTE]  
> The **LU Decomposition** (which Gauss Elimination implicitly generates) is a direct extension — the multipliers $m_{ik}$ form the matrix $L$, and the resulting upper triangular form is $U$. Hence, Gauss Elimination is the *engine* that powers LU factorization.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example

### 3.1 General Derivation of the Forward Elimination Step

Consider the original system $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ at step $k$, where the matrix has been partially reduced. The pivot is $a_{kk} \neq 0$. For each row $i = k+1, k+2, \ldots, n$:

**Step 1 — Define the multiplier for row $i$:**

$$m_{ik} = \frac{a_{ik}^{(k)}}{a_{kk}^{(k)}}$$

where the superscript $(k)$ denotes the matrix state at the start of step $k$.

**Step 2 — Apply elementary row operation $R_i \leftarrow R_i - m_{ik} R_k$:**

For $j = k, k+1, \ldots, n+1$ (where the $(n+1)$-th column is the augmented $\mathbf{b}$):

$$a_{ij}^{(k+1)} = a_{ij}^{(k)} - m_{ik} \cdot a_{kj}^{(k)}$$

**Step 3 — Verify the pivot column is zeroed below the diagonal:**

For $j = k$:

$$a_{ik}^{(k+1)} = a_{ik}^{(k)} - m_{ik} \cdot a_{kk}^{(k)} = a_{ik}^{(k)} - \frac{a_{ik}^{(k)}}{a_{kk}^{(k)}} \cdot a_{kk}^{(k)} = 0$$

This confirms $m_{ik}$ is chosen precisely to annihilate the sub-diagonal entry.

### 3.2 General Derivation of the Back Substitution Step

After $n-1$ elimination steps, the system becomes:

$$U\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{c}, \quad \text{where} \quad u_{ij} = a_{ij}^{(n-1)} \text{ for } i \leq j$$

The last equation is:

$$u_{nn} x_n = c_n \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x_n = \frac{c_n}{u_{nn}} = \frac{a_{n,n+1}^{(n-1)}}{a_{nn}^{(n-1)}}$$

For the $i$-th equation (where $i < n$):

$$\sum_{j=i}^{n} u_{ij} x_j = c_i$$

Isolating $x_i$:

$$u_{ii} x_i + \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} u_{ij} x_j = c_i$$

$$x_i = \frac{1}{u_{ii}} \left( c_i - \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} u_{ij} x_j \right) = \frac{1}{a_{ii}^{(n-1)}} \left( a_{i,n+1}^{(n-1)} - \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} a_{ij}^{(n-1)} x_j \right)$$

This is applied for $i = n-1, n-2, \ldots, 1$ in that order (since $x_{i+1}, x_{i+2}, \ldots, x_n$ are already known).

### 3.3 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** Solve the following $3 \times 3$ system using Gauss Elimination Method:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2x_1 + x_2 + x_3 &= 10 \\
3x_1 + 2x_2 + 3x_3 &= 18 \\
x_1 + 4x_2 + 9x_3 &= 16
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 0 — Form the Augmented Matrix $[A \vert \mathbf{b}]$:**

$$
\left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} 2 & 1 & 1 & 10 \\ 3 & 2 & 3 & 18 \\ 1 & 4 & 9 & 16 \end{array} \right]
$$

#### Phase I: Forward Elimination

**Step 1 (k = 1):** Pivot is $a_{11} = 2$. Compute multipliers for rows 2 and 3.

For row 2:

$$m_{21} = \frac{a_{21}}{a_{11}} = \frac{3}{2}$$

For row 3:

$$m_{31} = \frac{a_{31}}{a_{11}} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Apply $R_2 \leftarrow R_2 - m_{21} R_1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{22}^{\text{new}} &= a_{22} - m_{21} \cdot a_{12} = 2 - \frac{3}{2}(1) = 2 - 1.5 = 0.5 \\
a_{23}^{\text{new}} &= a_{23} - m_{21} \cdot a_{13} = 3 - \frac{3}{2}(1) = 3 - 1.5 = 1.5 \\
a_{24}^{\text{new}} &= a_{24} - m_{21} \cdot a_{14} = 18 - \frac{3}{2}(10) = 18 - 15 = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Apply $R_3 \leftarrow R_3 - m_{31} R_1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{32}^{\text{new}} &= a_{32} - m_{31} \cdot a_{12} = 4 - \frac{1}{2}(1) = 4 - 0.5 = 3.5 \\
a_{33}^{\text{new}} &= a_{33} - m_{31} \cdot a_{13} = 9 - \frac{1}{2}(1) = 9 - 0.5 = 8.5 \\
a_{34}^{\text{new}} &= a_{34} - m_{31} \cdot a_{14} = 16 - \frac{1}{2}(10) = 16 - 5 = 11
\end{aligned}
$$

**Matrix after Step 1:**

$$
\left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} 2 & 1 & 1 & 10 \\ 0 & 0.5 & 1.5 & 3 \\ 0 & 3.5 & 8.5 & 11 \end{array} \right]
$$

**Step 2 (k = 2):** Pivot is $a_{22} = 0.5$. Compute multiplier for row 3.

$$m_{32} = \frac{a_{32}}{a_{22}} = \frac{3.5}{0.5} = 7$$

**Apply $R_3 \leftarrow R_3 - m_{32} R_2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{33}^{\text{new}} &= a_{33} - m_{32} \cdot a_{23} = 8.5 - 7(1.5) = 8.5 - 10.5 = -2 \\
a_{34}^{\text{new}} &= a_{34} - m_{32} \cdot a_{24} = 11 - 7(3) = 11 - 21 = -10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Matrix after Step 2 (Upper Triangular Form):**

$$
\left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} 2 & 1 & 1 & 10 \\ 0 & 0.5 & 1.5 & 3 \\ 0 & 0 & -2 & -10 \end{array} \right]
$$

#### Phase II: Back Substitution

**Step 3 — Solve for $x_3$:**

$$x_3 = \frac{-10}{-2} = 5$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $x_2$:**

$$x_2 = \frac{1}{0.5} \left( 3 - 1.5 \cdot x_3 \right) = 2 \left( 3 - 1.5 \cdot 5 \right) = 2 \left( 3 - 7.5 \right) = 2(-4.5) = -9$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $x_1$:**

$$x_1 = \frac{1}{2} \left( 10 - 1 \cdot x_2 - 1 \cdot x_3 \right) = 0.5 \left( 10 - (-9) - 5 \right) = 0.5 \left( 10 + 9 - 5 \right) = 0.5 (14) = 7$$

**Final Solution Vector:**

$$\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 7 \\ -9 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2(7) + (-9) + 5 &= 14 - 9 + 5 = 10 \;\checkmark \\
3(7) + 2(-9) + 3(5) &= 21 - 18 + 15 = 18 \;\checkmark \\
1(7) + 4(-9) + 9(5) &= 7 - 36 + 45 = 16 \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Biomedical Interpretation:** In a **3-compartment pharmacokinetic model** (e.g., blood $\rightarrow$ liver $\rightarrow$ tissue), the variables $x_1, x_2, x_3$ could represent drug concentrations in these compartments. The negative concentration $x_2 = -9$ indicates that a physically meaningful solution requires additional constraints (e.g., non-negativity, or the model parameters need revision). This is a classic case where **linear systems in biomedical modeling** must be paired with **physiological constraints**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Algorithmic Flowchart — Gauss Elimination Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: System of n Linear Equations A times x equals b] --> B[Form Augmented Matrix n by n+1]
    B --> C[Initialize k = 1]
    C --> D{k less than n}
    D -- No --> M[Begin Back Substitution Phase]
    D -- Yes --> E{Is Pivot a_kk zero or near zero}
    E -- Yes --> F[Perform Row Interchange with row below]
    F --> G{Found a non-zero pivot below}
    G -- No --> H[Error: Singular Matrix, No Unique Solution]
    G -- Yes --> I[Update pivot with swapped row]
    E -- No --> I
    I --> J[For i from k+1 to n: compute multiplier m_ik = a_ik divided by a_kk]
    J --> K[For j from k to n+1: update a_ij = a_ij minus m_ik times a_kj]
    K --> L[Increment k = k + 1]
    L --> D
    M --> N[Compute x_n = a_n_n+1 divided by a_nn]
    N --> O[For i from n-1 down to 1: x_i equals 1 over a_ii times a_i_n+1 minus sum from j=i+1 to n of a_ij times x_j]
    O --> P[Output Solution Vector x1, x2, ..., xn]
    P --> Q[End]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Matrix Transformation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input["INPUT BLOCK"]
        A["Coefficient Matrix A n by n"]
        B["Constant Vector b n by 1"]
    end

    subgraph Form["AUGMENTATION BLOCK"]
        C["Augmented Matrix Aug = A vertical bar b, dimension n by n+1"]
    end

    subgraph Fwd["FORWARD ELIMINATION BLOCK"]
        D["Stage 1: Eliminate column 1 below pivot a11"]
        E["Stage 2: Eliminate column 2 below pivot a22"]
        F["Stage k: Eliminate column k below pivot akk"]
        G["Final: Upper Triangular Matrix U times x equals c"]
    end

    subgraph Back["BACK SUBSTITUTION BLOCK"]
        H["Compute xn from last row"]
        I["Iterate: Compute xi from i-th row using already known xj for j greater than i"]
        J["Solution Vector x"]
    end

    subgraph Bio["BIOMEDICAL APPLICATION LAYER"]
        K["Compartmental Pharmacokinetics"]
        L["ECG Lead-Field Analysis"]
        M["CT Image Reconstruction"]
    end

    A --> C
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I --> J
    J --> K
    J --> L
    J --> M
```

### 4.3 Memory Layout Diagram — $LU$ Decomposition Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P["PIVOTING MATRIX P"]
        P1["Row Permutation Record p1, p2, ..., pn"]
    end
    subgraph L["LOWER TRIANGULAR MATRIX L"]
        L1["Unit Diagonal: l_ii = 1"]
        L2["Sub-diagonal: l_ik = m_ik multiplier values"]
    end
    subgraph U["UPPER TRIANGULAR MATRIX U"]
        U1["Diagonal: u_ii = pivot values after elimination"]
        U2["Super-diagonal: u_ij for j greater than i"]
    end

    P1 --> L2
    L2 --> U1
    U1 --> U2
    L1 --> L2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Diagram Note:** The matrices $L$ and $U$ are products of the Gauss Elimination process: $PA = LU$. The multipliers $m_{ik}$ become entries of $L$, and the transformed matrix becomes $U$. This compact form is the foundation for efficient **multiple right-hand-side** solves in biomedical simulations (e.g., time-series ECG reconstruction).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]**

**Q1. [3 Marks] Define the Gauss Elimination Method and list its two main phases.**

**Model Answer:**  
The Gauss Elimination Method is a direct numerical technique for solving a system of $n$ simultaneous linear algebraic equations of the form $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$. The two main phases are:  
**(1) Forward Elimination:** The augmented matrix $[A \vert \mathbf{b}]$ is transformed into an upper triangular form by systematically eliminating variables below the main diagonal using elementary row operations.  
**(2) Back Substitution:** The unknowns are computed starting from the last equation (which has only one unknown) and proceeding backward to the first equation.  
(3 marks for clear definition + correct enumeration of both phases with brief explanation)

---

**Q2. [3 Marks] What is a "pivot element" in Gauss Elimination, and why might pivoting be necessary?**

**Model Answer:**  
A **pivot element** $a_{kk}$ is the diagonal entry of the coefficient matrix at step $k$ of the forward elimination process, used as the divisor to compute multipliers. **Pivoting** (interchanging rows or columns) becomes necessary when:  
(i) The pivot $a_{kk} = 0$, making division impossible.  
(ii) The pivot $\vert a_{kk} \vert$ is very small compared to other entries in the column, which can amplify rounding errors and lead to numerical instability.  
(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the two reasons)

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**Q.A. (a) [7 Marks]** Solve the following system of linear equations using the Gauss Elimination Method:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 + 2x_2 + x_3 &= 8 \\
2x_1 + 3x_2 + 4x_3 &= 20 \\
4x_1 + 3x_2 - 2x_3 &= 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**Augmented Matrix:**

$$
\left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} 1 & 2 & 1 & 8 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 & 20 \\ 4 & 3 & -2 & 8 \end{array} \right]
$$

**Step 1 ($k = 1$):** Pivot $= a_{11} = 1$.

Multipliers: $m_{21} = 2/1 = 2$, $m_{31} = 4/1 = 4$.

$R_2 \leftarrow R_2 - 2 R_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{22} &= 3 - 2(2) = -1 \\
a_{23} &= 4 - 2(1) = 2 \\
a_{24} &= 20 - 2(8) = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

$R_3 \leftarrow R_3 - 4 R_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{32} &= 3 - 4(2) = -5 \\
a_{33} &= -2 - 4(1) = -6 \\
a_{34} &= 8 - 4(8) = -24
\end{aligned}
$$

**Matrix after Step 1:**

$$
\left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} 1 & 2 & 1 & 8 \\ 0 & -1 & 2 & 4 \\ 0 & -5 & -6 & -24 \end{array} \right]
$$

**Step 2 ($k = 2$):** Pivot $= a_{22} = -1$.

Multiplier: $m_{32} = -5 / -1 = 5$.

$R_3 \leftarrow R_3 - 5 R_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{33} &= -6 - 5(2) = -16 \\
a_{34} &= -24 - 5(4) = -44
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Upper Triangular Form:**

$$
\left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} 1 & 2 & 1 & 8 \\ 0 & -1 & 2 & 4 \\ 0 & 0 & -16 & -44 \end{array} \right]
$$

**Back Substitution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_3 &= \frac{-44}{-16} = 2.75 \\
x_2 &= \frac{1}{-1} \left( 4 - 2(2.75) \right) = -(4 - 5.5) = -(-1.5) = 1.5 \\
x_1 &= 1 \cdot \left( 8 - 2(1.5) - 1(2.75) \right) = 8 - 3 - 2.75 = 2.25
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.25 \\ 1.5 \\ 2.75 \end{bmatrix}}$$

---

**Q.A. (b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **partial pivoting** with a suitable example and discuss its role in ensuring numerical stability.

**Model Answer:**

**Concept:** Partial pivoting is a row-interchange strategy applied at each elimination step $k$ to choose the row with the **largest absolute value** in column $k$ (among rows $k$ to $n$) as the pivot row. This minimizes the magnitude of multipliers and reduces rounding error propagation.

**Example:** Consider the system:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.0001 x_1 + x_2 &= 1 \\
x_1 + x_2 &= 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Without Pivoting:** Multiplier $m_{21} = 1 / 0.0001 = 10000$. The roundoff error becomes enormous, leading to an inaccurate solution.

**With Partial Pivoting:** Swap $R_1$ and $R_2$ so pivot becomes $1$ (largest in column 1). Multiplier becomes $m_{21} = 0.0001/1 = 0.0001$, which is small — resulting in a numerically stable computation.

**Role in Numerical Stability:**  
(i) Prevents division by zero or near-zero.  
(ii) Keeps multipliers bounded, reducing error magnification.  
(iii) Preserves the condition number — does not make a poorly-conditioned system well-conditioned, but prevents *artificial* ill-conditioning from poor pivot choices.  
(7 marks: 2 for concept, 3 for numerical example, 2 for stability discussion)

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]**

**Q.B. (a) [7 Marks]** Solve the following system using Gauss Elimination with **partial pivoting**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 x_1 + 2x_2 + x_3 &= 7 \\
4x_1 + 3x_2 - 2x_3 &= 8 \\
x_1 + 2x_2 + x_3 &= 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**Augmented Matrix:**

$$
\left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} 0 & 2 & 1 & 7 \\ 4 & 3 & -2 & 8 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 & 8 \end{array} \right]
$$

**Step 1 — Partial Pivoting in Column 1:** Largest $\vert a_{i1} \vert$ is $\vert 4 \vert$ in row 2. Swap $R_1 \leftrightarrow R_2$:

$$
\left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} 4 & 3 & -2 & 8 \\ 0 & 2 & 1 & 7 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 & 8 \end{array} \right]
$$

Pivot $= 4$. Multiplier: $m_{31} = 1/4 = 0.25$.

$R_3 \leftarrow R_3 - 0.25 R_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{32} &= 2 - 0.25(3) = 2 - 0.75 = 1.25 \\
a_{33} &= 1 - 0.25(-2) = 1 + 0.5 = 1.5 \\
a_{34} &= 8 - 0.25(8) = 8 - 2 = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Matrix after Step 1:**

$$
\left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} 4 & 3 & -2 & 8 \\ 0 & 2 & 1 & 7 \\ 0 & 1.25 & 1.5 & 6 \end{array} \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Partial Pivoting in Column 2:** Compare $\vert 2 \vert$ and $\vert 1.25 \vert$. Row 2 has the larger. No swap needed.

Pivot $= 2$. Multiplier: $m_{32} = 1.25/2 = 0.625$.

$R_3 \leftarrow R_3 - 0.625 R_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{33} &= 1.5 - 0.625(1) = 0.875 \\
a_{34} &= 6 - 0.625(7) = 6 - 4.375 = 1.625
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Upper Triangular Form:**

$$
\left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} 4 & 3 & -2 & 8 \\ 0 & 2 & 1 & 7 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.875 & 1.625 \end{array} \right]
$$

**Back Substitution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_3 &= \frac{1.625}{0.875} \approx 1.857 \\
x_2 &= \frac{7 - 1(1.857)}{2} = \frac{5.143}{2} \approx 2.571 \\
x_1 &= \frac{8 - 3(2.571) - (-2)(1.857)}{4} = \frac{8 - 7.714 + 3.714}{4} = \frac{4}{4} = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{x} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2.571 \\ 1.857 \end{bmatrix}}$$

---

**Q.B. (b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **computational complexity** of Gauss Elimination and its applications in **biomedical engineering**.

**Model Answer:**

**Computational Complexity:**  
For an $n \times n$ system, forward elimination requires:  
(i) Multiplication/division operations: $\frac{n^3 - n}{3} \approx \frac{n^3}{3}$  
(ii) Addition/subtraction operations: $\frac{n^3 - n}{3} \approx \frac{n^3}{3}$  
Back substitution requires $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ operations.  
**Total: $\mathcal{O}(n^3 / 3)$** — making it suitable for moderate-size systems but inefficient for very large sparse systems (where iterative methods are preferred).

**Applications in Biomedical Engineering:**

1. **ECG Forward Modeling (Lead-Field Equations):** Solves $V = L \mathbf{x}$ where $L$ is the lead-field matrix relating cardiac sources $\mathbf{x}$ to body-surface potentials $V$. Order: $n \approx 100$–$1000$ electrodes.

2. **CT Image Reconstruction (Algebraic Reconstruction Technique - ART):** Each iteration solves a sparse linear system. Gauss Elimination is used for the initial reconstruction of a low-resolution image.

3. **Compartmental Pharmacokinetics:** For a 3-compartment drug model, the steady-state concentrations are obtained by solving $A\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{d}$ where $A$ encodes inter-compartmental flow rates.

4. **Biomechanical Static FEA:** Joint force analysis in musculoskeletal models reduces to $K\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{F}$ where $K$ is the stiffness matrix.

5. **Inverse EEG/MEG Source Localization:** Solving $\Phi = G \cdot \mathbf{j}$ where $G$ is the gain matrix and $\mathbf{j}$ represents dipole moments.

(7 marks: 2 for complexity derivation, 5 for five applications with brief descriptions)

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (KTU Examiner Observations):**  
>  
> 1. **Forgetting to update the augmented column** when applying row operations — students often update only the coefficient matrix entries, losing **1–2 marks**.  
>  
> 2. **Arithmetic errors in multiplier computation** — using $a_{ik}/a_{kk}$ correctly is the single most important step; an error here cascades and **invalidates the entire solution** (loss of up to 5 marks).  
>  
> 3. **Back substitution in wrong order** — solving from top to bottom instead of bottom to top leads to **incorrect final values** (loss of 3–4 marks).  
>  
> 4. **Skipping verification** — KTU examiners appreciate a final substitution check; omitting it may cost **1 mark** of "presentation credit".  
>  
> 5. **Ignoring pivoting** when a zero pivot is encountered — students often "push through" with a zero pivot, leading to undefined multipliers (**2 marks lost**).  
>  
> 6. **Sign errors in the back-substitution formula** — the formula $x_i = (b_i - \sum a_{ij} x_j)/a_{ii}$ has a **minus sign** that students frequently omit.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]  
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Core Algorithm:** Two phases — *Forward Elimination* (reduce to upper triangular $U$) and *Back Substitution* (solve from bottom up).
- **Multiplier Formula:** $m_{ik} = a_{ik}/a_{kk}$, applied via $R_i \leftarrow R_i - m_{ik} R_k$.
- **Pivot Element:** The diagonal entry $a_{kk}$ used for elimination. Must be non-zero; **partial pivoting** is mandatory in real-world implementations.
- **Computational Cost:** $\mathcal{O}(n^3/3)$ flops for forward elimination + $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ for back substitution. Total $\approx n^3/3 + n^2$.
- **Partial Pivoting:** Swap the row with the **largest absolute value** in the pivot column into the pivot position.
- **Total Pivoting:** Swap both rows and columns (less common, more expensive).
- **LU Decomposition Link:** Multipliers $\rightarrow$ matrix $L$; pivoting matrix $P$; relation $PA = LU$. Determinant: $\det(A) = (-1)^p \cdot \prod u_{ii}$.
- **Condition Number Impact:** A well-conditioned system gives accurate solutions; ill-conditioned systems ($A$ nearly singular) are highly sensitive to data perturbation.
- **Biomedical Use-Cases:** ECG lead-field inversion, CT image reconstruction, pharmacokinetic compartmental models, biomechanical FEA, EEG/MEG source localization.
- **KTU Exam Tip:** Always present your work as a sequence of matrices; show the **augmented matrix** at every step — partial credit is awarded for correct intermediate steps.
- **Numerical Rule of Thumb:** If $\vert a_{kk} \vert$ is much smaller than other entries in its row/column, **always pivot** to maintain stability.
- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Dividing by the pivot AFTER row reduction (you divide the multipliers, then the constants separately) — keep them coupled in the row operation.
- **Format for KTU Answer Sheet:** Begin with the augmented matrix, then list each step clearly with $R_i \leftarrow R_i - m \cdot R_j$ notation, and conclude with back substitution boxed answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Eigenvalue Analysis - Eigenvectors and Eigenvalues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT634 - COMPUTATIONAL METHODS IN BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Eigenvalue Analysis - Eigenvectors and Eigenvalues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the rigorous language of linear algebra as prescribed by the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for *Computational Methods in Biomedical Engineering*, an **eigenvector** of a square matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is a non-zero column vector $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ whose direction remains invariant under the linear transformation induced by $A$. The corresponding **eigenvalue** $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$ (or $\mathbb{C}$) is the scalar factor by which the eigenvector is scaled during this transformation.

The defining canonical equation — the **Eigenvalue Equation** — is:

$$A\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$$

where:
- $A$ is the **system matrix** (e.g., stiffness matrix in FEA, covariance matrix in PCA, Hessian in optimization)
- $\mathbf{v} \neq \mathbf{0}$ is the **eigenvector** (right eigenvector)
- $\lambda$ is the **eigenvalue** (characteristic root, latent root)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (CO1 – KTU 2024):** Eigenvalue decomposition forms the computational backbone for *Principal Component Analysis (PCA)* in medical imaging (MRI/CT denoising), *modal analysis* of bone vibrations, and *stability analysis* of physiological control models. Mastery of $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$ is mandatory.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Stretching Rubber Sheet"

Imagine a flat rubber sheet stretched across a square frame. Now suppose you apply a *linear* shear-and-stretch transformation to it (this is your matrix $A$). Most points on the sheet will move to new positions AND change direction.

However, there will be a few special lines passing through the origin — these are the **eigenvectors**. After the transformation, every point on these lines remains *on the same line*; it only slides outward (or inward) along that line. The factor by which it slides outward is the **eigenvalue**.

> **Biomedical Intuition:** Think of an ECG signal segment as a sum of rhythmic "modes" (eigenvectors) each with a certain "energy" (eigenvalue). PCA extracts the dominant modes — those with the largest eigenvalues — to compress the signal while preserving clinical information.

## 1.3 Why Eigenvalues Are Non-Negotiable in Biomedical Engineering

| Biomedical Application | Role of Eigenvalue Analysis |
|---|---|
| **MRI/CT Image Compression** | PCA uses eigenvectors of the covariance matrix of image patches |
| **EEG Signal Decomposition** | Spectral decomposition via $A = Q \Lambda Q^T$ separates brain rhythms |
| **Bone Vibration Analysis** | Modal frequencies = $\sqrt{\lambda}$ of the stiffness/mass matrix system |
| **Cardiac Model Stability** | Eigenvalues of Jacobian determine whether heart rhythm converges |
| **Genomic Microarray Data** | Eigenvectors identify dominant gene expression patterns |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Metrics You Must Memorize:**
> - **Characteristic polynomial** degree = $n$ for an $n \times n$ matrix
> - **Trace theorem:** $\text{tr}(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$
> - **Determinant theorem:** $\det(A) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — How to Find Eigenvalues

The eigenvalue equation $A\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$ is rearranged as:

$$(A - \lambda I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$$

For a **non-trivial** solution ($\mathbf{v} \neq \mathbf{0}$), the matrix $(A - \lambda I)$ must be **singular** (non-invertible), i.e.,

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$$

This determinant, when expanded as a polynomial in $\lambda$, is called the **characteristic polynomial** $p(\lambda)$, and setting it to zero yields the **characteristic equation**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Flow

**Step 1 — Formulate the system matrix $A$.**
Identify the biomedical system (e.g., a $2 \times 2$ drug-compartment interaction model).

**Step 2 — Construct the characteristic matrix.**
Subtract $\lambda$ from each diagonal entry: form $(A - \lambda I)$.

**Step 3 — Set the determinant to zero.**
This gives a polynomial of degree $n$ in $\lambda$ (Cayley-Hamilton theorem compliant).

**Step 4 — Solve the polynomial.**
Use:
- Quadratic formula for $n = 2$
- Cubic formula or numerical methods for $n = 3$
- QR algorithm / power iteration for $n \geq 4$

**Step 5 — Back-substitute each $\lambda_i$ into $(A - \lambda_i I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$.**
Solve the homogeneous system to obtain the eigenvector $\mathbf{v}_i$ (up to scaling).

## 2.3 Algebraic Properties (Must-Know)

| Property | Statement | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Spectral theorem** (symmetric $A$) | $A = Q \Lambda Q^T$, $Q$ orthogonal | PCA in imaging |
| **Diagonalizability** | $A = P D P^{-1}$ if $n$ linearly independent eigenvectors exist | Decoupling ODE systems |
| **Distinct eigenvalues ⟹ independent eigenvectors** | Always true | Guaranteed basis construction |
| **Similarity invariance** | Eigenvalues of $P^{-1}AP$ = eigenvalues of $A$ | Change of basis in FE meshes |
| **Powers of $A$** | $A^k \mathbf{v} = \lambda^k \mathbf{v}$ | Recursive neural population dynamics |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is your **exam-day survival kit** for Module 1.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Eigenvalue equation | $A\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$ | Foundation stone |
| 2 | Characteristic equation | $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$ | Yields $n$-degree polynomial |
| 3 | Eigenvector equation | $(A - \lambda I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$ | Null space computation |
| 4 | Trace identity | $\sum \lambda_i = \text{tr}(A)$ | Quick check for arithmetic |
| 5 | Determinant identity | $\prod \lambda_i = \det(A)$ | Second quick check |
| 6 | Spectral decomposition (symmetric $A$) | $A = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i \mathbf{q}_i \mathbf{q}_i^T$ | Used in PCA |
| 7 | Quadratic eigenvalues | $\lambda = \dfrac{\text{tr}(A) \pm \sqrt{[\text{tr}(A)]^2 - 4\det(A)}}{2}$ | For $2 \times 2$ matrices |
| 8 | Cayley-Hamilton theorem | $p(A) = 0$ where $p(\lambda) = \det(\lambda I - A)$ | Matrix equation technique |
| 9 | Rayleigh quotient | $R(A, \mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{\mathbf{x}^T A \mathbf{x}}{\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{x}}$ | Bounds extreme eigenvalues |
| 10 | Condition number | $\kappa(A) = \dfrac{\vert \lambda_{\max} \vert}{\vert \lambda_{\min} \vert}$ | Numerical stability indicator |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** In *finite element biomechanics*, the equation $K\mathbf{u} = \lambda M \mathbf{u}$ (where $K$ = stiffness matrix, $M$ = mass matrix) is the **generalized eigenvalue problem**. Its eigenvalues $\lambda_i = \omega_i^2$ give the natural vibration frequencies of bones, arterial walls, or the vocal tract — directly used in designing prosthetics and diagnosing stenoses.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Characteristic Equation (General $n \times n$ Case)

Starting from the eigenvalue equation:

$$A\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$$

Subtract $\lambda \mathbf{v}$ from both sides:

$$A\mathbf{v} - \lambda \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$$

Factor out $\mathbf{v}$ (valid because matrix multiplication distributes over vector subtraction via the identity matrix):

$$(A - \lambda I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Why must $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$?**

If $\det(A - \lambda I) \neq 0$, then $(A - \lambda I)$ is invertible, and we can left-multiply both sides by $(A - \lambda I)^{-1}$ to obtain $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$ — the trivial solution. But by definition eigenvectors must be **non-zero**, so the only way a non-trivial solution exists is if $(A - \lambda I)$ is singular:

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$$

## 3.2 Worked Example — $2 \times 2$ Drug Interaction Matrix

Let the system matrix be:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 1 — Form the characteristic matrix $(A - \lambda I)$:**

$$A - \lambda I = \begin{bmatrix} 4 - \lambda & 1 \\ 2 & 3 - \lambda \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the determinant:**

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = (4 - \lambda)(3 - \lambda) - (1)(2)$$

**Step 3 — Expand using FOIL:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(4 - \lambda)(3 - \lambda) &= 4 \cdot 3 - 4\lambda - 3\lambda + \lambda^2 \\
&= 12 - 7\lambda + \lambda^2
\end{aligned}
$$

So:

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = 12 - 7\lambda + \lambda^2 - 2$$

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = \lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 10$$

**Step 4 — Set determinant to zero and factor:**

$$\lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 10 = 0$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 10 &= (\lambda - 5)(\lambda - 2) \\
\therefore \lambda_1 &= 5, \quad \lambda_2 = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify using trace and determinant identities (sanity check):**

- $\text{tr}(A) = 4 + 3 = 7$ ✓ equals $\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = 5 + 2 = 7$
- $\det(A) = (4)(3) - (1)(2) = 10$ ✓ equals $\lambda_1 \cdot \lambda_2 = 5 \times 2 = 10$

**Step 6 — Find eigenvector for $\lambda_1 = 5$:**

Substitute into $(A - \lambda_1 I)\mathbf{v}_1 = \mathbf{0}$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 4 - 5 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 - 5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_{11} \\ v_{12} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 2 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_{11} \\ v_{12} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Row 2 is just $-2 \times$ Row 1, so we have one independent equation:

$$-v_{11} + v_{12} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_{11} = v_{12}$$

Take $v_{12} = 1$, then:

$$\mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7 — Find eigenvector for $\lambda_2 = 2$:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} 4 - 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 - 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_{21} \\ v_{22} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_{21} \\ v_{22} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

The single equation is:

$$2 v_{21} + v_{22} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_{22} = -2 v_{21}$$

Take $v_{21} = 1$, then:

$$\mathbf{v}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example — $3 \times 3$ Symmetric Matrix (Imaging Covariance)

Let:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

(This is the tridiagonal Laplacian-like matrix commonly arising in 1-D biomechanical chains.)

**Step 1 — Characteristic determinant:**

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = \det\begin{bmatrix} 2-\lambda & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 2-\lambda & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 2-\lambda \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Expand along Row 1 using cofactor expansion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\det(A - \lambda I) &= (2-\lambda)\bigl[(2-\lambda)(2-\lambda) - (-1)(-1)\bigr] \\
&\quad -(-1)\bigl[(-1)(2-\lambda) - (-1)(0)\bigr] \\
&\quad + 0 \cdot (\cdots)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify each term:**

First term (the $(2-\lambda)$ minor):

$$(2-\lambda)^2 - 1 = (2-\lambda)^2 - 1$$

Let $x = 2-\lambda$ for clarity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x^2 - 1 &= (x-1)(x+1) \\
&= (1-\lambda)(3-\lambda)
\end{aligned}
$$

So first term contribution: $(2-\lambda)(1-\lambda)(3-\lambda)$.

Second term: $+1 \cdot \bigl[-(2-\lambda) - 0\bigr] = -(2-\lambda)$

Combining:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\det(A - \lambda I) &= (2-\lambda)(1-\lambda)(3-\lambda) - (2-\lambda) \\
&= (2-\lambda)\bigl[(1-\lambda)(3-\lambda) - 1\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

Expand the inner bracket:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1-\lambda)(3-\lambda) &= 3 - \lambda - 3\lambda + \lambda^2 \\
&= 3 - 4\lambda + \lambda^2
\end{aligned}
$$

So:

$$(1-\lambda)(3-\lambda) - 1 = \lambda^2 - 4\lambda + 2$$

Therefore the full characteristic polynomial is:

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = (2-\lambda)(\lambda^2 - 4\lambda + 2)$$

**Step 4 — Solve the quadratic factor:**

$$\lambda^2 - 4\lambda + 2 = 0$$

$$\lambda = \frac{4 \pm \sqrt{16 - 8}}{2} = \frac{4 \pm \sqrt{8}}{2} = 2 \pm \sqrt{2}$$

**Step 5 — List all eigenvalues:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_1 &= 2 + \sqrt{2} \approx 3.414 \\
\lambda_2 &= 2 \\
\lambda_3 &= 2 - \sqrt{2} \approx 0.586
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification using trace:**

$$\text{tr}(A) = 2 + 2 + 2 = 6$$

$$\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 = (2 + \sqrt{2}) + 2 + (2 - \sqrt{2}) = 6 \quad ✓$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Eigenvalue Computation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given square matrix A of size n x n] --> B[Form the characteristic matrix A minus lambda I]
    B --> C[Compute determinant det of A minus lambda I]
    C --> D[Set det = 0 to obtain characteristic polynomial p of lambda]
    D --> E{n equals 2?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Use quadratic formula]
    E -- No --> G{n equals 3?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Use cubic formula or synthetic division]
    G -- No --> I[Apply numerical methods QR or Power Iteration]
    F --> J[Obtain eigenvalue list lambda 1 through lambda n]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[For each lambda i substitute into A minus lambda i I times v equals 0]
    K --> L[Solve homogeneous linear system via row reduction]
    L --> M[Assign one free variable and express all others]
    M --> N[Normalise eigenvector: v i divided by its norm]
    N --> O[Output complete eigendecomposition]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of PCA in Biomedical Imaging

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StageA[Stage 1: Data Acquisition]
        A1[MRI Scan Slice Stack] --> A2[Vectorise each patch into column]
        A2 --> A3[Form data matrix X of size p times N]
    end

    subgraph StageB[Stage 2: Covariance Construction]
        A3 --> B1[Mean centre each row of X]
        B1 --> B2[Compute covariance matrix Sigma equals 1 over N times X X transpose]
    end

    subgraph StageC[Stage 3: Eigendecomposition]
        B2 --> C1[Form characteristic equation det Sigma minus lambda I equals 0]
        C1 --> C2[Solve for eigenvalues lambda 1 greater than lambda 2 greater than ... greater than lambda p]
        C2 --> C3[Compute eigenvectors q 1 through q p]
    end

    subgraph StageD[Stage 4: Projection and Compression]
        C3 --> D1[Select top k eigenvectors forming Q k]
        D1 --> D2[Project data: Y equals Q k transpose X]
        D2 --> D3[Reconstruct approximation: X hat equals Q k Y]
    end

    D3 --> E[Compressed medical image with minimal diagnostic loss]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Mapping — Eigenvectors as Invariant Directions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Matrix A acting as linear transformation] -->|applied to| V1[Eigenvector v 1: direction unchanged]
    M -->|applied to| V2[Eigenvector v 2: direction unchanged]
    M -->|applied to| Vn[Eigenvector v n: direction unchanged]
    V1 -->|stretched by| L1[Lambda 1]
    V2 -->|stretched by| L2[Lambda 2]
    Vn -->|stretched by| Ln[Lambda n]
    L1 --> OUT[Full spectral decomposition A equals Q Lambda Q transpose]
    L2 --> OUT
    Ln --> OUT
    OUT --> APP[Biomedical applications: PCA, modal analysis, ECG spectral decomposition]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define eigenvalues and eigenvectors of a square matrix. State the characteristic equation used to determine the eigenvalues.**

**Model Answer:**

For a square matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, a non-zero vector $\mathbf{v}$ is called an eigenvector if $A\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$ for some scalar $\lambda$. The scalar $\lambda$ is called the corresponding eigenvalue.

The eigenvalues are obtained as roots of the **characteristic equation**:

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$$

This determinant, when expanded, yields an $n$-degree polynomial in $\lambda$ whose $n$ roots (possibly complex or repeated) are the eigenvalues of $A$. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and prove the trace and determinant identities for eigenvalues.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:**
- $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i = \text{tr}(A)$
- $\prod_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i = \det(A)$

**Proof sketch:** The characteristic polynomial can be written as:

$$p(\lambda) = \lambda^n - (\text{tr}\,A)\lambda^{n-1} + \cdots + (-1)^n \det(A)$$

By Vieta's formulas, the sum of roots (eigenvalues) equals the coefficient of $\lambda^{n-1}$ taken with opposite sign, which is $\text{tr}(A)$. The product of roots equals $(-1)^n \det(A)$ divided by the leading coefficient $(-1)^n$, giving $\det(A)$. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 1 Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** For the biomedical compartmental matrix $A = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$ representing a two-compartment drug distribution model, find all eigenvalues and the corresponding eigenvectors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Verify the spectral theorem by expressing $A$ as $Q \Lambda Q^T$ where $Q$ is orthogonal and $\Lambda$ is diagonal. Explain its significance in dimensionality reduction for ECG signal features. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1 — Characteristic equation:**

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = \det\begin{bmatrix} 5-\lambda & 4 \\ 1 & 2-\lambda \end{bmatrix} = (5-\lambda)(2-\lambda) - 4$$

**Step 2 — Expand:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(5-\lambda)(2-\lambda) &= 10 - 5\lambda - 2\lambda + \lambda^2 \\
&= 10 - 7\lambda + \lambda^2
\end{aligned}
$$

So:

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = \lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 10 - 4 = \lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 6$$

**Step 3 — Factor:**

$$\lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 6 = (\lambda - 6)(\lambda - 1) = 0$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_1 = 6, \quad \lambda_2 = 1}$$

**Step 4 — Eigenvector for $\lambda_1 = 6$:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 4 \\ 1 & -4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_{11} \\ v_{12} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Row 1: $-v_{11} + 4 v_{12} = 0 \Rightarrow v_{11} = 4 v_{12}$.

Choosing $v_{12} = 1$:

$$\mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Eigenvector for $\lambda_2 = 1$:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 4 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_{21} \\ v_{22} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Row 1: $4 v_{21} + 4 v_{22} = 0 \Rightarrow v_{21} = -v_{22}$.

Choosing $v_{22} = 1$:

$$\mathbf{v}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(7 marks — full allocation)**

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1 — Normalise eigenvectors.**

For $\mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$: $\|\mathbf{v}_1\| = \sqrt{16 + 1} = \sqrt{17}$.

$$\mathbf{q}_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{17}}\begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

For $\mathbf{v}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$: $\|\mathbf{v}_2\| = \sqrt{1 + 1} = \sqrt{2}$.

$$\mathbf{q}_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Verify orthogonality:**

$$\mathbf{q}_1^T \mathbf{q}_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{17}\sqrt{2}}[(4)(-1) + (1)(1)] = \frac{-3}{\sqrt{34}} \neq 0$$

**Re-orthogonalise using Gram-Schmidt** since $A$ is not symmetric. For a general $2 \times 2$ matrix with distinct real eigenvalues, $A = P D P^{-1}$ (diagonalisation), not $Q \Lambda Q^T$. So strictly speaking the spectral theorem requires $A$ to be **symmetric**.

**Adjusted Approach:** Replace $A$ by its symmetric part $A_s = \dfrac{A + A^T}{2}$:

$$A_s = \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 10 & 5 \\ 5 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 2.5 \\ 2.5 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Recompute eigenvalues of $A_s$:

$$\det(A_s - \lambda I) = (5-\lambda)(2-\lambda) - 6.25 = \lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 3.75$$

$$\lambda = \frac{7 \pm \sqrt{49 - 15}}{2} = \frac{7 \pm \sqrt{34}}{2}$$

$$\lambda_+ \approx 6.415, \quad \lambda_- \approx 0.585$$

Since $A_s$ is symmetric, eigenvectors are orthogonal — the spectral theorem $A_s = Q \Lambda Q^T$ applies.

**Significance in ECG feature reduction:**
- ECG signals contain redundant correlated features (R-R interval, QRS amplitude, ST slope, etc.)
- The symmetric covariance matrix $A_s$ captures pairwise feature correlations
- The eigenvector $\mathbf{q}_+$ (corresponding to $\lambda_+$) defines the **principal axis** of maximum variance — projects ECG beats onto a single feature
- Achieves dimensionality reduction while preserving $>90\%$ of diagnostic variance for arrhythmia detection

**(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]` — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Find the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of the symmetric matrix $A = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$ used in a coupled-oscillator model of two interacting bone segments. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two eigenvalues of a $3 \times 3$ matrix are $\lambda_1 = 2$ and $\lambda_2 = -1$. If $\text{tr}(A) = 5$ and one eigenvector is $\mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$, determine the third eigenvalue and justify using the trace theorem. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1 — Characteristic equation:**

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = (3-\lambda)^2 - 1 = 0$$

**Step 2 — Expand:**

$$(3-\lambda)^2 = 1 \Rightarrow 3 - \lambda = \pm 1$$

$$\lambda_1 = 2, \quad \lambda_2 = 4$$

**Step 3 — Eigenvector for $\lambda_1 = 2$:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_{11} \\ v_{12} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Row 1: $v_{11} + v_{12} = 0 \Rightarrow v_{11} = -v_{12}$.

$$\mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Eigenvector for $\lambda_2 = 4$:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_{21} \\ v_{22} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Row 1: $-v_{21} + v_{22} = 0 \Rightarrow v_{21} = v_{22}$.

$$\mathbf{v}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Note orthogonality:**

$\mathbf{v}_1^T \mathbf{v}_2 = (1)(1) + (-1)(1) = 0$ ✓ (expected for symmetric matrix)

**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1 — Apply trace theorem:**

$$\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 = \text{tr}(A) = 5$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values:**

$$2 + (-1) + \lambda_3 = 5$$

**Step 3 — Solve:**

$$\lambda_3 = 5 - 1 = 4$$

**Step 4 — Justification:**

The trace identity holds universally for any $n \times n$ matrix because the coefficient of $\lambda^{n-1}$ in the characteristic polynomial (with sign flipped) is always the trace. The eigenvector $\mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$ can be verified: $A\mathbf{v}_1 = 2\mathbf{v}_1$ must hold for some $A$ with these eigenvalues — this information constrains the matrix but does not affect $\lambda_3$ which is uniquely determined by the trace.

$$\boxed{\lambda_3 = 4}$$

**(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign convention:** Many students write $\det(\lambda I - A) = 0$ instead of $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$. Both are valid but the eigenvalues are the same roots; do not mix them in mid-problem.
> 2. **Skipping normalisation:** In Part B questions worth 7 marks, examiners demand at least a *mention* of normalised eigenvectors when applying the spectral theorem.
> 3. **Confusing "trace" with "transpose":** In valuation scripts, the trace $\text{tr}(A)$ is frequently miswritten — remember it is the *sum of diagonal elements*, not row sums.
> 4. **Forgetting to check singularity:** When solving $(A - \lambda I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$, always state explicitly that $(A - \lambda I)$ is singular so a non-trivial solution exists.
> 5. **Algebra slip in $3 \times 3$ determinants:** Use cofactor expansion or row reduction — never attempt direct formula unless absolutely confident.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1: Eigenvalue Analysis**

- **Core equation:** $A\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$, $\mathbf{v} \neq \mathbf{0}$
- **Characteristic equation:** $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$ yields an $n$-degree polynomial in $\lambda$
- **For $2 \times 2$ matrix** $\begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}$:
  - Eigenvalues: $\lambda = \dfrac{(a+d) \pm \sqrt{(a-d)^2 + 4bc}}{2}$
  - Trace identity: $a + d = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2$
  - Determinant identity: $ad - bc = \lambda_1 \lambda_2$
- **Symmetric matrix ⟹ orthogonal eigenvectors ⟹ spectral theorem $A = Q\Lambda Q^T$ applies**
- **Distinct eigenvalues ⟹ linearly independent eigenvectors** (no guarantee of orthogonality unless symmetric)
- **Powers of $A$:** $A^k \mathbf{v} = \lambda^k \mathbf{v}$ — vital for recursive biological models
- **Biomedical applications to recall:** PCA for MRI compression, modal analysis of bone vibrations, EEG/HRV spectral decomposition, stability analysis via Jacobian eigenvalues
- **Cayley-Hamilton theorem:** Every square matrix satisfies its own characteristic polynomial $p(A) = 0$
- **Condition number** $\kappa(A) = \vert \lambda_{\max} / \lambda_{\min} \vert$ — large values indicate ill-conditioned biomedical inverse problems
- **Exam trap:** Repeated eigenvalues may yield fewer than $n$ independent eigenvectors → check by computing $\text{nullity}(A - \lambda I)$
- **Last-mile mantra:** *Trace = sum of eigenvalues; Determinant = product of eigenvalues.* Use this for instant sanity checks on every KTU numerical.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Eigenvalue Problems in Biomedical Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT634 - COMPUTATIONAL METHODS IN BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Eigenvalue Problems in Biomedical Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

An **eigenvalue problem** is a linear algebra problem of the form:

$$A\mathbf{x} = \lambda \mathbf{x}$$

where $A$ is a square $n \times n$ matrix, $\mathbf{x}$ is a non-zero vector called the **eigenvector**, and $\lambda$ is a scalar called the **eigenvalue**. The matrix $A$ typically arises from discretizing a continuous physical, biological, or signal-processing operator.

In **biomedical engineering**, eigenvalue problems arise naturally in:

- Vibration and stability analysis of **biomechanical structures** (bones, prostheses, tissues).
- **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** of biomedical signals such as EEG, ECG, and EMG.
- **Modal analysis** of the cardiovascular system (pulse wave propagation).
- **Spectral analysis** of biomedical images (tumor detection, tissue segmentation).
- Solving **population dynamics** models in epidemiology (e.g., age-structured disease spread).
- Eigenvalue-based **stability assessment** of biochemical reaction networks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 1 of *PEBMT634*, eigenvalue problems are introduced as the foundational tool for analyzing linear biomedical systems where the natural modes (oscillations, dominant patterns, principal directions of variance) must be extracted from matrix representations of physiological data or mechanical structures.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are **listening to the heart sounds** of a patient through a stethoscope. The heart produces a complex mixture of sounds — S1, S2, murmurs, background noise — all jumbled together. Now, think of this complex mixture as a **matrix** $A$. What you really want is to identify the **pure, fundamental components** of that sound — the dominant rhythm, the clean valve closures, the precise frequencies that characterize the patient's specific cardiac signature.

The **eigenvectors** are those pure, characteristic patterns hidden inside the messy signal. The **eigenvalues** are the corresponding strengths — telling you how much each pure pattern contributes to what you actually hear.

In short:

- **Matrix $A$** = The noisy, mixed biomedical signal/system.
- **Eigenvector $\mathbf{x}$** = The pure underlying physiological pattern.
- **Eigenvalue $\lambda$** = The amplification factor (how prominent that pattern is).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical constants / metrics used in biomedical eigenvalue analysis:**
> - **Dominant Frequency ($\lambda$)** in Hz, representing the principal rhythm of ECG/EEG.
> - **Variance Explained (%)** by each principal component in PCA.
> - **Natural Frequency ($\omega_n = \sqrt{\lambda}$)** in rad/s for biomechanical vibrations.
> - **Damping Ratio ($\zeta$)** for oscillatory biomedical systems.

## 1.3 The Characteristic Equation

The eigenvalues are obtained by solving:

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$$

This determinant yields an $n^{th}$-degree polynomial (the **characteristic polynomial**), whose roots are the eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n$. For each $\lambda_i$, the corresponding eigenvector $\mathbf{x}_i$ is found by solving:

$$(A - \lambda_i I)\mathbf{x}_i = \mathbf{0}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Spectral Decomposition):** If $A$ is a real symmetric matrix (as in PCA covariance matrices of biomedical signals), then $A$ has $n$ real eigenvalues and $n$ mutually orthogonal eigenvectors. This is the backbone of biomedical signal feature extraction.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

### 2.1.1 Why Eigenvalue Problems Matter in Biomedicine

Most biomedical systems, when linearized, can be written as $\dot{\mathbf{y}} = A\mathbf{y}$.
