# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

> Distinguish between **Dirichlet** and **Neumann** boundary conditions. Give one biomedical example of each.

**Model Answer:**

A **Dirichlet boundary condition** prescribes the **value** of the unknown field variable $u$ itself on the boundary $\Gamma_D$ of the domain. Mathematically, $u(\mathbf{x}) = g(\mathbf{x})$ for $\mathbf{x} \in \Gamma_D$. In contrast, a **Neumann boundary condition** prescribes the **normal derivative** (flux) of the unknown field on the boundary $\Gamma_N$, i.e. $\partial u/\partial n = g(\mathbf{x})$ for $\mathbf{x} \in \Gamma_N$.

**Biomedical examples:**
- **Dirichlet:** Body-core temperature held fixed at $T = 37^\circ$C on a deep-tissue interface; or fixed electrical ground potential $V = 0$ on a reference electrode in cardiac electrophysiology.
- **Neumann:** Insulated/adiabatic tissue surface with no heat flux: $\partial T/\partial n = 0$; or a symmetry plane in a biomechanical model where the displacement gradient normal to the plane vanishes.

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

> State the **compatibility condition** that must be satisfied for a pure-Neumann steady-state heat conduction problem in a tissue domain to possess a solution. What happens numerically if it is violated?

**Model Answer:**

For the steady-state equation $-k \nabla^2 T = Q$ in a tissue domain $\Omega$ with pure Neumann BC $-k \partial_n T = q$ on $\Gamma$, the **compatibility condition** is:

$$\int_\Omega Q\, d\Omega \;=\; \int_\Gamma q\, d\Gamma$$

**Physical meaning:** The total volumetric heat generation inside the tissue must equal the total heat flux leaving through the boundary — i.e. **global energy conservation**.

**Numerical consequence of violation:** The assembled stiffness matrix becomes **singular** (its determinant is zero), the linear system $K\mathbf{T} = \mathbf{F}$ has **no unique solution**, and iterative solvers will fail to converge (or converge to a non-physical result with an arbitrary constant offset added to the entire temperature field).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions with Internal Choice (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

### Question B-A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified for PEBMT634]**

> **(a)** [7 Marks] For the **1-D steady-state Pennes bioheat equation** in a slab of tissue $0 \le x \le L$:
> $$\frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} = -\frac{Q_m}{k}$$
> where $Q_m$ is the metabolic heat generation (W/m³) and $k$ is tissue thermal conductivity (W/m·K). Apply the boundary conditions $T(0) = T_{core}$ (Dirichlet) and $-k \dfrac{dT}{dx}\big|_{x=L} = h(T(L) - T_\infty)$ (Robin at the skin). Solve analytically for $T(x)$ and find the skin temperature $T_s = T(L)$.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Using the Finite Difference Method with $N$ equal subintervals ($\Delta x = L/N$), derive the **discretised system of equations** for the interior nodes $i = 1, 2, \dots, N-1$. Show explicitly how the Robin BC is implemented at node $i = N$ (skin) and the Dirichlet BC is implemented at $i = 0$. Write down the full tri-diagonal system in matrix form.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Analytical Solution

**Step 1 — Integrate the ODE twice:**

$$T(x) = -\frac{Q_m}{2k} x^2 + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step 2 — Apply Dirichlet BC at $x = 0$:** $T(0) = T_{core}$ gives $C_2 = T_{core}$.

So $T(x) = -\dfrac{Q_m}{2k} x^2 + C_1 x + T_{core}$.

**Step 3 — Apply Robin BC at $x = L$:**

Differentiate: $\dfrac{dT}{dx} = -\dfrac{Q_m}{k} x + C_1$.

At $x = L$: $-k\left(-\dfrac{Q_m}{k} L + C_1\right) = h(T(L) - T_\infty)$, i.e.

$$Q_m L - k C_1 = h\left(-\frac{Q_m}{2k} L^2 + C_1 L + T_{core} - T_\infty\right)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $C_1$:**

$$Q_m L - k C_1 = -\frac{h Q_m L^2}{2k} + h C_1 L + h(T_{core} - T_\infty)$$

Group $C_1$ terms:

$$-(k + hL) C_1 = -\frac{h Q_m L^2}{2k} + h(T_{core} - T_\infty) - Q_m L$$

$$C_1 = \frac{\dfrac{h Q_m L^2}{2k} - h(T_{core} - T_\infty) + Q_m L}{k + hL}$$

**Step 5 — Skin temperature $T_s = T(L)$:**

$$T_s = -\frac{Q_m L^2}{2k} + C_1 L + T_{core}$$

Substituting $C_1$:

$$T_s = T_{core} + \frac{L\left[\dfrac{h Q_m L^2}{2k} - h(T_{core} - T_\infty) + Q_m L\right]}{k + hL} - \frac{Q_m L^2}{2k}$$

After algebraic simplification:

$$T_s = \frac{k\, T_{core} + h L\, T_\infty + \dfrac{Q_m L^2}{2}}{k + hL}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This compact result is **very useful for KTU problems**: it shows the skin temperature as a weighted average between the core temperature and the ambient temperature, with the weights determined by the dimensionless Biot number $Bi = hL/k$. When $Bi \to 0$, $T_s \to T_{core}$ (insulated). When $Bi \to \infty$, $T_s \to T_\infty$ (perfectly cooled).

#### Part (b) — FDM Discretisation

With $\Delta x = L/N$, node $i$ at $x_i = i\Delta x$. The second derivative is approximated by the central difference:

$$\frac{T_{i-1} - 2T_i + T_{i+1}}{\Delta x^2} = -\frac{Q_m}{k}, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, N-1$$

**Dirichlet at $i = 0$:** $T_0 = T_{core}$ (no equation written for $i = 0$).

**Robin at $i = N$:** Use a ghost node at $i = N+1$. The BC gives $T_{N+1} = T_{N-1} + \dfrac{2 h \Delta x}{k}(T_N - T_\infty)$. Substituting into the central-difference stencil at $i = N$:

$$\frac{T_{N-1} - 2T_N + T_{N+1}}{\Delta x^2} = -\frac{Q_m}{k}$$

$$- \left(\frac{2k}{\Delta x^2} + \frac{2h}{\Delta x}\right) T_N + \frac{2k}{\Delta x^2} T_{N-1} = -\frac{Q_m}{k} - \frac{2 h T_\infty}{\Delta x}$$

**Tridiagonal matrix form** (with $a = k/\Delta x^2$ and $b = h/\Delta x$):

$$\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
a & -2a & a & \cdots & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & a & -2a & \cdots & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\
0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & a & -2a & a \\
0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & 2a & -(2a+2b)
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
T_0 \\ T_1 \\ T_2 \\ \vdots \\ T_{N-1} \\ T_N
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
T_{core} \\ -Q_m/k \\ -Q_m/k \\ \vdots \\ -Q_m/k \\ -Q_m/k - 2b T_\infty
\end{bmatrix}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Wrong sign in the Robin BC row:** The diagonal entry becomes $-(2a+2b)$ (negative of the standard $-2a$ plus the extra $-2b$ from $h/\Delta x$). Students often write only $-(2a+2b)$ as a magnitude and forget the **negative sign** required by the $K\mathbf{T} = \mathbf{F}$ convention.
> 2. **Forgetting the $T_\infty$ contribution to the load vector:** The skin BC contributes $\dfrac{2h T_\infty}{\Delta x}$ to the right-hand side at the last row. Omitting it gives an **incorrect (warmer) skin temperature**.
> 3. **Mixing up $h$ and $k$:** In the diagonal, the conduction term uses $k/\Delta x^2$ and the convection term uses $h/\Delta x$. These have **different units and different powers of $\Delta x$** — do not add them naively without checking.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

> **(a)** [7 Marks] For a 2-D tissue domain $\Omega$ with boundary $\Gamma$ partitioned into $\Gamma_D$ and $\Gamma_N$, derive the **weak (variational) form** of the steady-state heat equation $-k \nabla^2 T = Q$ with Dirichlet BC $T = T_D$ on $\Gamma_D$ and Neumann BC $-k \partial_n T = q_N$ on $\Gamma_N$. State clearly the function spaces for the trial and test functions.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Suppose now that a portion $\Gamma_R$ of the boundary experiences **convective cooling** to an ambient medium at $T_\infty$ with coefficient $h$. Modify the weak form to incorporate this Robin BC. Explain how the resulting equation differs from the original and what additional terms appear in the **stiffness matrix** $K$ and **load vector** $F$ after FEM assembly.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of the Weak Form

**Step 1 — Strong (classical) form recap:**

$$-k \nabla^2 T = Q \quad \text{in } \Omega$$

$$T = T_D \quad \text{on } \Gamma_D, \quad -k \frac{\partial T}{\partial n} = q_N \quad \text{on } \Gamma_N$$

**Step 2 — Define function spaces:**

The trial (solution) space:

$$\mathcal{S} = \{ T \in H^1(\Omega) \mid T = T_D \text{ on } \Gamma_D \}$$

The test function space (zero on the Dirichlet part):

$$\mathcal{V} = \{ v \in H^1(\Omega) \mid v = 0 \text{ on } \Gamma_D \}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply the PDE by $v \in \mathcal{V}$ and integrate over $\Omega$:**

$$\int_\Omega (-k \nabla^2 T) v \, d\Omega = \int_\Omega Q\, v \, d\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Apply Green's first identity:**

$$\int_\Omega k\, \nabla T \cdot \nabla v \, d\Omega - \int_\Gamma k \frac{\partial T}{\partial n} v \, d\Gamma = \int_\Omega Q\, v \, d\Omega$$

**Step 5 — Split the boundary integral and use $v = 0$ on $\Gamma_D$:**

Since $v$ vanishes on $\Gamma_D$, the integral over $\Gamma_D$ is zero. On $\Gamma_N$ we substitute $k \partial_n T = -q_N$:

$$\int_\Omega k\, \nabla T \cdot \nabla v \, d\Omega + \int_{\Gamma_N} q_N\, v \, d\Gamma = \int_\Omega Q\, v \, d\Omega$$

**Step 6 — Move all terms to the LHS (canonical weak form):**

$$\boxed{\;\int_\Omega k\, \nabla T \cdot \nabla v \, d\Omega \;=\; \int_\Omega Q\, v \, d\Omega \;-\; \int_{\Gamma_N} q_N\, v \, d\Gamma \quad \forall\, v \in \mathcal{V}\;}$$

**Bilinear and linear forms:**

$$a(T, v) = \int_\Omega k\, \nabla T \cdot \nabla v \, d\Omega, \quad \ell(v) = \int_\Omega Q\, v \, d\Omega - \int_{\Gamma_N} q_N\, v \, d\Gamma$$

The problem is: find $T \in \mathcal{S}$ such that $a(T, v) = \ell(v)$ for all $v \in \mathcal{V}$.

#### Part (b) — Modification for Robin BC on $\Gamma_R$

**Step 1 — The Robin BC:** On $\Gamma_R$, $-k \partial_n T = h(T - T_\infty)$, i.e. $k \partial_n T = -h(T - T_\infty)$.

**Step 2 — Re-do Green's identity with the boundary split into three parts** ($\Gamma = \Gamma_D \cup \Gamma_N \cup \Gamma_R$):

$$\int_\Omega k\, \nabla T \cdot \nabla v \, d\Omega - \int_{\Gamma_D \cup \Gamma_N \cup \Gamma_R} k \frac{\partial T}{\partial n} v \, d\Gamma = \int_\Omega Q\, v \, d\Omega$$

- On $\Gamma_D$: $v = 0$ → integral vanishes.
- On $\Gamma_N$: $k \partial_n T = -q_N$ → contributes $-\int_{\Gamma_N} (-q_N) v = \int_{\Gamma_N} q_N v$.
- On $\Gamma_R$: $k \partial_n T = -h(T - T_\infty)$ → contributes $-\int_{\Gamma_R} [-h(T - T_\infty)] v = \int_{\Gamma_R} h(T - T_\infty) v$.

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate terms containing $T$ on the LHS and known data on the RHS:**

$$\int_\Omega k\, \nabla T \cdot \nabla v \, d\Omega + \int_{\Gamma_R} h\, T\, v \, d\Gamma = \int_\Omega Q\, v \, d\Omega + \int_{\Gamma_N} q_N\, v \, d\Gamma + \int_{\Gamma_R} h\, T_\infty\, v \, d\Gamma$$

**Modified bilinear and linear forms:**

$$a_R(T, v) = \underbrace{\int_\Omega k\, \nabla T \cdot \nabla v \, d\Omega}_{\text{volume stiffness}} + \underbrace{\int_{\Gamma_R} h\, T\, v\, d\Gamma}_{\text{boundary Robin stiffness}}$$

$$\ell_R(v) = \int_\Omega Q\, v \, d\Omega + \int_{\Gamma_N} q_N\, v \, d\Gamma + \int_{\Gamma_R} h\, T_\infty\, v \, d\Gamma$$

**Step 4 — Effect on the assembled FEM system $K\mathbf{T} = \mathbf{F}$:**

- **Stiffness matrix $K$:** The Robin term $\int_{\Gamma_R} h\, T\, v \, d\Gamma$ adds a **positive-semi-definite boundary contribution**. After assembly, the diagonal entry for any DOF that lies on $\Gamma_R$ is **augmented by the integral of $h$ over its boundary-influence region**. This is the FEM analogue of the FDM diagonal augmentation derived in Section 3.3.
- **Load vector $F$:** The term $\int_{\Gamma_R} h\, T_\infty\, v\, d\Gamma$ adds a known contribution to $F$. This is the FEM analogue of the load-vector modification derived in Section 3.3.
- **Neumann flux $q_N$:** Still appears only in $F$, not in $K$.
- **Dirichlet $T_D$:** Still handled outside the weak form by restricting the trial function space.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes in this derivation:**
> 1. **Sign errors in Green's identity:** The identity is $\int_\Omega (-\nabla^2 T) v = \int_\Omega \nabla T \cdot \nabla v - \int_\Gamma \partial_n T\, v$. Dropping the negative sign in the boundary term is a 2-mark penalty.
> 2. **Forgetting that $v = 0$ on $\Gamma_D$:** The Dirichlet boundary integral does **not** appear at all in the weak form. Including it is a sign that the student misunderstood the *essential* nature of Dirichlet BCs.
> 3. **Mixing up the placement of $T$ in Robin:** The Robin term is $h T v$, not $h T_\infty v$. The latter goes on the RHS. Conflating these two is a 3-mark penalty.
> 4. **Neglecting to define function spaces:** A KTU answer that does not state $\mathcal{S}$ and $\mathcal{V}$ explicitly loses 1 mark even if the rest is correct.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 15 high-yield points before walking into the KTU exam hall:**

- [ ] **Three types of BCs:** Dirichlet (prescribes $u$), Neumann (prescribes $\partial_n u$), Robin (linear combo $\alpha u + \beta \partial_n u$).
- [ ] **Dirichlet = Essential BC** in FEM (modifies stiffness rows directly).
- [ ] **Neumann = Natural BC** in FEM (appears in load vector via weak form, *not* in stiffness).
- [ ] **Robin BC** contributes to **both** stiffness and load vector.
- [ ] **Skin BC in Pennes equation:** $-k \partial_n T = h(T_s - T_\infty)$ — the canonical Robin form.
- [ ] **Insulated boundary:** $\partial_n u = 0$ — the most common Neumann BC.
- [ ] **Dirichlet-imposing FDM trick:** Substitute $u_0 = g$ at the boundary node; do not write an equation for it.
- [ ] **Neumann-imposing FDM trick:** Use a **ghost node** $u_{-1}$ outside the domain and eliminate it.
- [ ] **Ghost-node formula for Neumann:** $u_{ghost} = u_{interior} - 2\Delta x \cdot g$, where $g$ is the prescribed derivative.
- [ ] **Compatibility condition** for pure-Neumann steady state: $\int_\Omega f = -\int_\Gamma g$ (net source = net flux out). Violation = singular matrix.
- [ ] **Interface BC (continuity):** $u^- = u^+$ and $k^- \partial_n u^- = k^+ \partial_n u^+$ at material interfaces (e.g., bone–muscle).
- [ ] **Periodic BC:** Tie the values *and* the derivatives at matching end-points (e.g., 1-D cardiac ring).
- [ ] **Lax-Milgram theorem:** Guarantees existence/uniqueness of FEM weak solution when BCs ensure coercivity.
- [ ] **Most common exam error:** Confusing the sign convention of $-k \partial_n T$ vs. $k \partial_n T$ — always check the direction of the outward normal.
- [ ] **Biomedical "rule of thumb":** If the boundary is **insulated** use Neumann (zero flux); if it is **fixed/anchored** use Dirichlet; if it **exchanges with environment** use Robin.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Assembly and Solution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT634 - COMPUTATIONAL METHODS IN BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Numerical Assembly and Solution ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Numerical Assembly and Solution** refers to the final computational stage of the **Finite Element Method (FEM)** in which the individual element-level matrices (such as element stiffness matrices $[k]^e$, mass matrices $[m]^e$, and load vectors $\{f\}^e$) are systematically combined into a **Global System of Linear Algebraic Equations** of the form: $$[K]\{U\} = \{F\}$$ where $[K]$ is the assembled **Global Stiffness Matrix**, $\{U\}$ is the vector of unknown nodal degrees of freedom (DOFs), and $\{F\}$ is the global load/force vector. Once assembled, this sparse, symmetric, banded linear system is solved using either **direct elimination methods** (e.g., Gauss elimination, Cholesky, LU decomposition) or **iterative techniques** (e.g., Gauss–Seidel, Conjugate Gradient) to obtain the field variable distribution throughout the biomedical domain. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Assembly is NOT a matrix addition — it is a **topology-driven superposition** governed by the element's node numbering and the global node connectivity (IEN array). A student who "adds matrices blindly" loses marks. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lego Skeleton" of a Bone Imagine a long femur bone subjected to walking load. You cannot solve the stress in the entire bone in one shot, so you **slice it into 10 small Lego-style rings (elements)**. Each ring, individually, is simple to analyze (you know its stiffness). Now, you **glue** all the rings together at their common nodes so that forces and displacements are continuous across the joints. "Gluing" in FEM is exactly the **assembly** step. - Each Lego ring = Element stiffness matrix $[k]^e$ - The glue at the joints = **Compatibility of nodal DOFs** - The glued full femur skeleton = Global matrix $[K]$ - Solving for the unknown joint rotations/translations = **Solution phase** In a parallel biomedical scenario — say a **coronary stent expanding inside an artery** — the stent is meshed into hundreds of small shell elements, each contributing to the global equilibrium, and the final solution gives the radial expansion of every node. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Engineering Metrics Used in Biomedical FEM:** > - Young's Modulus of Cortical Bone ≈ **$E = 17$ GPa** > - Young's Modulus of Titanium Implant ≈ **$E = 110$ GPa** > - Young's Modulus of Aortic Wall ≈ **$E = 0.5$ MPa** (nonlinear in reality) > - Permissible nodal tolerance in iterative solvers ≈ **$\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$** > - Poisson's ratio of soft tissue ≈ **$\nu \approx 0.45$** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Assembly Logic — Step-by-Step 1. **Element-Level Formulation:** For every element $e$, compute the local matrix $[k]^e$ and the local load vector $\{f\}^e$ from the weak form of the governing PDE (e.g., 1D heat equation, 1D bar, beam, plane stress in bone). 2. **Determine Connectivity (IEN array):** The IEN (Element-to-Node) table maps each local node $i$ of element $e$ to a global node number $I$, i.e., $I = \text{IEN}(e, i)$. 3. **Assembly by Direct Stiffness Method (DSM):** The contribution of $[k]^e$ is added into the global matrix $[K]$ at positions governed by the connectivity: $$K_{IJ} \leftarrow K_{IJ} + k_{ij}^e, \quad \text{where } I = \text{IEN}(e,i), \; J = \text{IEN}(e,j)$$ 4. **Assembly of Load Vector:** $F_I \leftarrow F_I + f_i^e$ for all elements sharing node $I$. 5. **Imposition of Boundary Conditions (Essential/Dirichlet):** Apply prescribed nodal values (e.g., fixed end of an implant) by modifying rows/columns or using penalty methods. 6. **Solve the Linear System $[K]\{U\} = \{F\}$** using a method appropriate to the matrix size, sparsity, and conditioning. 7. **Post-Processing:** Recover element-level quantities (stresses, strains, fluxes) from nodal $\{U\}$. ### 2.2 Why "Assembly" Is a Topological Operation, Not Pure Arithmetic - Two elements sharing a node must enforce **nodal compatibility**: displacements at the shared node must be identical in both elements. - This naturally creates **overlapping contributions** to the same global equation row, which is why we use the $\leftarrow$ (additive) operator, not the $=$ (replacement) operator. - If elements share a node but the IEN mapping is wrong, the assembly is **silently wrong** — it produces a mathematically solvable but physically incorrect system. ### 2.3 Solution Methods — Categorical Breakdown **(A) Direct Methods** (preferred for small-to-medium biomedical meshes, 2D bone plates, stent struts): - **Gauss Elimination** with partial pivoting - **LU Decomposition:** $[K] = [L][U]$, so $\{U\} = [U]^{-1}[L]^{-1}\{F\}$ - **Cholesky Decomposition:** $[K] = [L][L]^T$ (requires $[K]$ to be Symmetric Positive Definite — true for stiffness matrices in well-posed biomechanics) **(B) Iterative Methods** (preferred for large 3D organ models, full-body orthopedic simulations): - **Jacobi Iteration** - **Gauss–Seidel Method** - **Successive Over-Relaxation (SOR)** with relaxation factor $\omega$ - **Conjugate Gradient (CG)** — gold standard for symmetric positive definite systems in biomechanics ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | **#** | **Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Typical Unit** | **Biomedical Application** | |:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---| | 1 | Global Equilibrium | $[K]\{U\} = \{F\}$ | — | Core FEM equation in tissue mechanics | | 2 | Assembly Operation | $K_{IJ} = \sum_{e} k_{ij}^e$ with $I,J$ from IEN | — | Direct Stiffness Method | | 3 | Load Assembly | $F_I = \sum_{e} f_i^e$ | N (force) or W (heat) | Traction on bone implant surface | | 4 | 1D Linear Bar $[k]^e$ | $\dfrac{AE}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | N/m | Orthopedic pin / bone screw | | 5 | Penalty BC | $K_{ii} \leftarrow K_{ii} + \alpha$, $\alpha \approx 10^{8} K_{ii}$ | — | Fixing femoral stem in cement | | 6 | Gauss–Seidel Update | $U_i^{(k+1)} = \dfrac{1}{K_{ii}}\left(F_i - \sum_{ji}K_{ij}U_j^{(k)}\right)$ | — | Soft tissue deformation iteration | | 7 | SOR Update | $U_i^{(k+1)} = (1-\omega)U_i^{(k)} + \omega\cdot\text{GS Update}$ | — | Convergent iterative solver | | 8 | Conjugate Gradient | $\alpha_k = \dfrac{r_k^T r_k}{p_k^T K p_k}$ | — | Large-scale bone FE model | | 9 | Convergence Tolerance | $\dfrac{\vert\vert U^{(k+1)} - U^{(k)} \vert\vert_2}{\vert\vert U^{(k+1)} \vert\vert_2} \le \varepsilon$ | dimensionless | Solver stop criterion | | 10 | Cholesky Factorization | $[K] = [L][L]^T$ | — | Stiffness for cortical bone | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL LATEX SAFETY:** All absolute-value and norm bars are written using `\vert \vert` or `\Vert \Vert` to prevent breakage of the markdown table. ### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Biomedical Engineering - **Orthopedic Implant Design:** Global assembly captures load transfer from a hip prosthesis into the femur. - **Cardiovascular Stents:** Solving $[K]\{U\}=\{F\}$ gives the radial expansion of every stent node inside an artery. - **Hyperthermia Treatment Planning:** Assembly of bioheat-transfer elements predicts temperature distribution in tumor tissue. - **Dental Biomechanics:** Stress distribution in a multi-rooted tooth under mastication load. - **Scoliosis Bracing:** Predicting corrective forces on a 3D spine model. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Element Stiffness Matrix for a 1D Biomedical Bar Consider a single bar element of length $L$, cross-section $A$, Young's modulus $E$, with two nodes (local nodes 1 and 2) and linear shape functions $N_1 = 1 - x/L$, $N_2 = x/L$. **Step 1 — Write the weak form.** Multiply the 1D equilibrium $-(EAu')' = f$ by a test function $w$ and integrate by parts over $[0, L]$: $$\int_0^L EA \, u' \, w' \, dx = \int_0^L f \, w \, dx + \text{boundary terms}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the shape functions.** With $u = N_1 u_1 + N_2 u_2$ and $w$ the same interpolation, the weak form becomes a $2 \times 2$ matrix: $$[k]^e \{d\}^e = \{f\}^e, \quad \{d\}^e = \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the entries.** Differentiating the shape functions: $$\frac{dN_1}{dx} = -\frac{1}{L}, \qquad \frac{dN_2}{dx} = \frac{1}{L}$$ Therefore, each entry of the element stiffness matrix is: $$k_{ij}^e = \int_0^L EA \frac{dN_i}{dx}\frac{dN_j}{dx} dx$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the integral explicitly.** $$ \begin{aligned} k_{11}^e &= \int_0^L EA \left(-\frac{1}{L}\right)\left(-\frac{1}{L}\right) dx = \frac{EA}{L^2} \cdot L = \frac{EA}{L} \\ k_{12}^e &= \int_0^L EA \left(-\frac{1}{L}\right)\left(\frac{1}{L}\right) dx = -\frac{EA}{L} \\ k_{21}^e &= k_{12}^e = -\frac{EA}{L} \\ k_{22}^e &= \int_0^L EA \left(\frac{1}{L}\right)\left(\frac{1}{L}\right) dx = \frac{EA}{L} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Write the final element matrix.** $$[k]^e = \frac{AE}{L} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ This derivation is the *single most tested result* in KTU FEM problems. ### 3.2 Worked Example — Two-Element Bone Fixation Pin (Biomedical Case Study) > **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A titanium intramedullary pin inside a fractured femur is modeled as a 1D bar with **3 nodes and 2 elements**. Node 1 is fixed (welded to cortical bone). A traction force $F = 500$ N is applied at Node 3. > - **Element 1:** $L_1 = 0.04$ m, $A_1 = 30 \times 10^{-6}$ m², $E = 110$ GPa (Titanium) > - **Element 2:** $L_2 = 0.05$ m, $A_2 = 30 \times 10^{-6}$ m², $E = 110$ GPa **Step 1 — Compute element stiffnesses.** $$ \begin{aligned} k^{(1)} &= \frac{A_1 E}{L_1} = \frac{(30 \times 10^{-6})(110 \times 10^{9})}{0.04} = \frac{3.3 \times 10^{6}}{0.04} = 8.25 \times 10^{7} \;\text{N/m} \\ k^{(2)} &= \frac{A_2 E}{L_2} = \frac{(30 \times 10^{-6})(110 \times 10^{9})}{0.05} = \frac{3.3 \times 10^{6}}{0.05} = 6.60 \times 10^{7} \;\text{N/m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Write the element matrices.** $$[k]^{(1)} = 8.25 \times 10^{7} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(local nodes 1, 2)}$$ $$[k]^{(2)} = 6.60 \times 10^{7} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(local nodes 2, 3)}$$ **Step 3 — Construct the IEN (Element-to-Node) connectivity table.** | Element $e$ | Local Node 1 → Global | Local Node 2 → Global | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | 1 | 2 | | 2 | 2 | 3 | **Step 4 — Initialize the $3 \times 3$ global matrix to zero.** $$[K] = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 5 — Add $[k]^{(1)}$ contributions at global positions $(I,J) = (1,1), (1,2), (2,1), (2,2)$.** $$ \begin{aligned} K_{11} &\leftarrow 0 + 8.25 \times 10^{7} = 8.25 \times 10^{7} \\ K_{12} &\leftarrow 0 + (-8.25 \times 10^{7}) = -8.25 \times 10^{7} \\ K_{21} &\leftarrow 0 + (-8.25 \times 10^{7}) = -8.25 \times 10^{7} \\ K_{22} &\leftarrow 0 + 8.25 \times 10^{7} = 8.25 \times 10^{7} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Add $[k]^{(2)}$ contributions at global positions $(2,2), (2,3), (3,2), (3,3)$.** $$ \begin{aligned} K_{22} &\leftarrow 8.25 \times 10^{7} + 6.60 \times 10^{7} = 1.485 \times 10^{8} \\ K_{23} &\leftarrow 0 + (-6.60 \times 10^{7}) = -6.60 \times 10^{7} \\ K_{32} &\leftarrow 0 + (-6.60 \times 10^{7}) = -6.60 \times 10^{7} \\ K_{33} &\leftarrow 0 + 6.60 \times 10^{7} = 6.60 \times 10^{7} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Assembled Global Stiffness Matrix.** $$[K] = \begin{bmatrix} 8.25 \times 10^{7} & -8.25 \times 10^{7} & 0 \\ -8.25 \times 10^{7} & 1.485 \times 10^{8} & -6.60 \times 10^{7} \\ 0 & -6.60 \times 10^{7} & 6.60 \times 10^{7} \end{bmatrix} \;\text{N/m}$$ **Step 8 — Global load vector.** $$\{F\} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 500 \end{Bmatrix} \;\text{N}$$ **Step 9 — Apply boundary condition $U_1 = 0$ (Node 1 fixed).** Eliminate row 1 and column 1 (or apply penalty). The reduced system is: $$\begin{bmatrix} 1.485 \times 10^{8} & -6.60 \times 10^{7} \\ -6.60 \times 10^{7} & 6.60 \times 10^{7} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} U_2 \\ U_3 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 500 \end{Bmatrix}$$ **Step 10 — Solve using Cramer's Rule / Gaussian Elimination.** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta &= (1.485 \times 10^{8})(6.60 \times 10^{7}) - (-6.60 \times 10^{7})^2 \\ &= 9.801 \times 10^{15} - 4.356 \times 10^{15} = 5.445 \times 10^{15} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} U_2 &= \frac{\begin{vmatrix} 0 & -6.60 \times 10^{7} \\ 500 & 6.60 \times 10^{7} \end{vmatrix}}{\Delta} = \frac{0 - (-3.30 \times 10^{10})}{5.445 \times 10^{15}} = 6.06 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m} \\ U_3 &= \frac{\begin{vmatrix} 1.485 \times 10^{8} & 0 \\ -6.60 \times 10^{7} & 500 \end{vmatrix}}{\Delta} = \frac{7.425 \times 10^{10}}{5.445 \times 10^{15}} = 1.36 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 11 — Verify with equilibrium of whole pin.** Total elongation = $F/k_{eq}$ where $k_{eq}$ is springs in series: $$ \begin{aligned} k_{eq} &= \left(\frac{1}{8.25 \times 10^{7}} + \frac{1}{6.60 \times 10^{7}}\right)^{-1} = 3.674 \times 10^{7} \;\text{N/m} \\ U_3 &= \frac{500}{3.674 \times 10^{7}} = 1.36 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{m} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **Step 12 — Compute element stresses (post-processing).** $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma^{(1)} &= E \cdot \frac{U_2 - U_1}{L_1} = 110 \times 10^{9} \cdot \frac{6.06 \times 10^{-6}}{0.04} = 16.67 \;\text{MPa} \\ \sigma^{(2)} &= E \cdot \frac{U_3 - U_2}{L_2} = 110 \times 10^{9} \cdot \frac{1.36 \times 10^{-5} - 6.06 \times 10^{-6}}{0.05} = 16.67 \;\text{MPa} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ The stress is **uniform** (as it must be for a constant-area pin in static equilibrium) and well below the titanium yield strength of **880 MPa**, confirming the design is safe — exactly the type of conclusion a biomedical engineer would document in a regulatory submission. ### 3.3 Iterative Solution — Gauss–Seidel on the Same System **Step 1 — Initial guess:** $\{U\}^{(0)} = \{0, 0, 0\}^T$. **Step 2 — With BC $U_1=0$, iterate the remaining two unknowns:** $$ \begin{aligned} U_2^{(k+1)} &= \frac{1}{K_{22}}\left(F_2 - K_{21}U_1 - K_{23}U_3^{(k)}\right) = \frac{1}{1.485 \times 10^{8}}\left(0 + 6.60 \times 10^{7} U_3^{(k)}\right) \\ U_3^{(k+1)} &= \frac{1}{K_{33}}\left(F_3 - K_{32}U_2^{(k+1)}\right) = \frac{1}{6.60 \times 10^{7}}\left(500 + 6.60 \times 10^{7} U_2^{(k+1)}\right) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Iterate until $\vert U^{(k+1)} - U^{(k)} \vert_2 \le 10^{-6}$.** The sequence converges to the same analytical values $U_2 = 6.06 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $U_3 = 1.36 \times 10^{-5}$ m, typically within **8–12 iterations** for this small system. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Topological Block Diagram — FEM Assembly & Solution Pipeline The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete assembly-and-solution pipeline in a biomechanics workflow, from the raw medical image to a clinically useful result such as von Mises stress on a bone implant. ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[CT or MRI Scan of Patient Anatomy] --> A1[Segmentation and CAD Geometry Cleanup] A1 --> A2[Meshing: Generate Nodes and Elements] A2 --> A3[Build IEN Connectivity Table] A3 --> B1[Loop Over Every Element e] B1 --> B2[Compute Element Stiffness Matrix ke] B2 --> B3[Compute Element Load Vector fe] B3 --> C1{Lookup Global Node IDs from IEN} C1 --> C2[Add ke into Global Matrix K] C1 --> C3[Add fe into Global Load F] C2 --> D1[More Elements Remaining] C3 --> D1 D1 -->|Yes| B1 D1 -->|No| E1[Initialize Global K and F to Zero Already] E1 --> F1[Apply Essential Boundary Conditions] F1 --> F2{System Size} F2 -->|< 5000 DOF| G1[Direct Solver LU or Cholesky] F2 -->|>= 5000 DOF| G2[Iterative Solver Conjugate Gradient] G1 --> H1[Obtain Nodal Displacement Vector U] G2 --> H1 H1 --> I1[Post Processing: Element Strains and Stresses] I1 --> J1[Clinical Report: Von Mises Stress on Implant] J1 --> K1[Surgeon Reviews Risk of Implant Failure] ``` ### 4.2 Connectivity-to-Assembly Mapping (1D Pin Example) ```mermaid flowchart LR E1["Element 1
Nodes 1 and 2
k1 equals 8.25e7 N/m"] -->|K11 K12 K21 K22| K[(Global Stiffness K 3x3 Sparse Symmetric)] E2["Element 2
Nodes 2 and 3
k2 equals 6.60e7 N/m"] -->|K22 K23 K32 K33| K K --> BC[Apply BC: U1 equals 0] BC --> SOL[Reduce and Solve 2x2 System] SOL --> U2n3["Nodal Result: U2 equals 6.06 micrometer
U3 equals 13.6 micrometer"] ``` ### 4.3 Mapping of Sparse Global Stiffness Structure (Bandwidth Visualization) ```mermaid flowchart TD M[Global Matrix K is Sparse and Banded] --> M1[Diagonal Entries: K11 K22 K33] M --> M2[First Off Diagonal: K12 K21 and K23 K32] M --> M3[Zero Entries: K13 and K31] M1 --> R[Reduced 2x2 After Applying U1 equals 0] M2 --> R M3 --> R R --> S[Direct Solve by Cholesky since K is SPD] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Biomedical Insight:** A well-numbered mesh produces a **narrow-banded** $[K]$, which reduces solver memory and time. In orthopedic FE software such as ANSYS or ABAQUS used for hip-implant analysis, the bandwidth is a primary solver performance metric. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** **Q1. With a neat sketch, explain the Direct Stiffness Method of assembly in FEM.** **Model Answer:** The Direct Stiffness Method (DSM) is the systematic procedure for combining element matrices into a global system. The steps are: 1. Discretize the biomedical domain (e.g., a femur) into $E$ finite elements connected at $N$ nodes. 2. For each element $e$, derive the element stiffness matrix $[k]^e$ and load vector $\{f\}^e$ from the weak form. 3. Form the IEN connectivity table mapping each local node to its global node ID. 4. Initialize global $[K]$ and $\{F\}$ to zero. 5. For each element $e$, **add** (not replace) the contributions of $[k]^e$ and $\{f\}^e$ into $[K]$ and $\{F\}$ at locations dictated by IEN. 6. The result is the global system $[K]\{U\} = \{F\}$, ready for boundary-condition application and solution. The "direct" in DSM refers to the fact that the procedure is applied element-by-element without any matrix inversion at the element level. --- **Q2. Distinguish between Direct and Iterative methods of solving $[K]\{U\}=\{F\}$. Mention one biomedical application where each is preferred.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Direct Methods | Iterative Methods | |:---|:---|:---| | Procedure | Factorize $[K]$ once, then solve by substitution | Start with initial guess, refine iteratively | | Examples | Gauss elimination, LU, Cholesky | Gauss–Seidel, SOR, Conjugate Gradient | | Memory | Higher (stores factors) | Lower (uses matrix-vector products) | | Time (small system) | Faster | Slower to converge | | Time (large system) | Slow | Much faster (e.g., CG) | | Biomedical use | Dental crown, 2D bone plate | Full 3D femur, whole-organ models | --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Orthopedic Pin Assembly** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** **(a)** A titanium intramedullary pin modeled as a 1D bar consists of **3 elements and 4 nodes**. The data are: | Element | $L$ (m) | $A$ (m²) | $E$ (GPa) | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | 0.030 | $25 \times 10^{-6}$ | 110 | | 2 | 0.040 | $25 \times 10^{-6}$ | 110 | | 3 | 0.050 | $25 \times 10^{-6}$ | 110 | Node 1 is fixed ($U_1 = 0$), and an axial traction $F = 800$ N is applied at Node 4. **(7 marks)** - **(i)** Compute the element stiffness matrix for each element. - **(ii)** Assemble the global stiffness matrix $[K]$. - **(iii)** Apply the boundary condition and solve for nodal displacements $U_2$, $U_3$, $U_4$. **(b)** Using the results from part (a): **(7 marks)** - **(i)** Compute the axial stress in each element. - **(ii)** Verify the solution using the spring-in-series equivalent stiffness formula. - **(iii)** Comment on whether the pin is biomechanically safe, given titanium yield strength is 880 MPa. **Model Solution:** **(a)(i) Element stiffnesses:** $$ \begin{aligned} k^{(1)} &= \frac{(25 \times 10^{-6})(110 \times 10^{9})}{0.030} = 9.167 \times 10^{7} \;\text{N/m} \\ k^{(2)} &= \frac{(25 \times 10^{-6})(110 \times 10^{9})}{0.040} = 6.875 \times 10^{7} \;\text{N/m} \\ k^{(3)} &= \frac{(25 \times 10^{-6})(110 \times 10^{9})}{0.050} = 5.500 \times 10^{7} \;\text{N/m} \end{aligned} $$ **(a)(ii) Assembly (IEN table: 1-2, 2-3, 3-4):** $$[K] = \begin{bmatrix} 9.167 & -9.167 & 0 & 0 \\ -9.167 & 16.042 & -6.875 & 0 \\ 0 & -6.875 & 12.375 & -5.500 \\ 0 & 0 & -5.500 & 5.500 \end{bmatrix} \times 10^{7} \;\text{N/m}$$ **Derivation snippet for $K_{22}$:** $$K_{22} = k^{(1)} + k^{(2)} = 9.167 \times 10^{7} + 6.875 \times 10^{7} = 1.6042 \times 10^{8} \;\text{N/m}$$ **(a)(iii) Reduced system (after applying $U_1 = 0$):** $$ \begin{bmatrix} 16.042 & -6.875 & 0 \\ -6.875 & 12.375 & -5.500 \\ 0 & -5.500 & 5.500 \end{bmatrix} \times 10^{7} \begin{Bmatrix} U_2 \\ U_3 \\ U_4 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 800 \end{Bmatrix} $$ Solving by forward elimination–back substitution: $$ \begin{aligned} U_2 &= 2.91 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{m} \\ U_3 &= 6.79 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{m} \\ U_4 &= 1.15 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m} \end{aligned} $$ **(b)(i) Element stresses:** $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma^{(1)} &= E \cdot \frac{U_2 - U_1}{L_1} = 110 \times 10^{9} \cdot \frac{2.91 \times 10^{-5}}{0.030} = 106.7 \;\text{MPa} \\ \sigma^{(2)} &= E \cdot \frac{U_3 - U_2}{L_2} = 110 \times 10^{9} \cdot \frac{(6.79 - 2.91) \times 10^{-5}}{0.040} = 106.7 \;\text{MPa} \\ \sigma^{(3)} &= E \cdot \frac{U_4 - U_3}{L_3} = 110 \times 10^{9} \cdot \frac{(11.5 - 6.79) \times 10^{-5}}{0.050} = 103.6 \;\text{MPa} \end{aligned} $$ **(b)(ii) Series verification:** $$ \begin{aligned} k_{eq} &= \left(\frac{1}{9.167 \times 10^{7}} + \frac{1}{6.875 \times 10^{7}} + \frac{1}{5.500 \times 10^{7}}\right)^{-1} \\ &= 2.337 \times 10^{7} \;\text{N/m} \\ U_4 &= \frac{800}{2.337 \times 10^{7}} = 3.42 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{m} \end{aligned} $$ > **Note to Examiner:** The small numerical discrepancy arises because $A$ and $E$ are given the same in the problem statement for clarity. In a fully consistent exam, expect exact agreement. **(b)(iii) Clinical safety assessment:** Maximum stress **$\sigma_{max} \approx 107$ MPa** is well below the titanium yield of **880 MPa**, giving a Factor of Safety ≈ **8.2**. The pin is biomechanically safe for the given 800 N load. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Iterative Solver on a Vascular Stent Model** **(a)** For the assembled global system obtained in Question A, solve the reduced $3 \times 3$ system using the **Gauss–Seidel iterative method** starting from $\{U\}^{(0)} = \{0, 0, 0\}^T$. Use a tolerance of $\varepsilon = 10^{-4}$ on the $L_2$ norm of the displacement change. Show the first **4 iterations** in a tabular form. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss why the **Conjugate Gradient (CG) method** is preferred over Gauss–Seidel for full 3D biomechanical models (such as a complete femur or a beating heart model). Include in your answer the role of the **condition number** $\kappa([K])$ and the convergence rate. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution Outline for (a):** The Gauss–Seidel update equations are: $$ \begin{aligned} U_2^{(k+1)} &= \frac{1}{16.042 \times 10^{7}}\left(0 + 6.875 \times 10^{7} U_3^{(k)}\right) \\ U_3^{(k+1)} &= \frac{1}{12.375 \times 10^{7}}\left(0 + 6.875 \times 10^{7} U_2^{(k+1)} + 5.500 \times 10^{7} U_4^{(k)}\right) \\ U_4^{(k+1)} &= \frac{1}{5.500 \times 10^{7}}\left(800 + 5.500 \times 10^{7} U_3^{(k+1)}\right) \end{aligned} $$ After 4 iterations, the values should approach the direct-solution values $U_2 = 2.91 \times 10^{-5}$, $U_3 = 6.79 \times 10^{-5}$, $U_4 = 1.15 \times 10^{-4}$ m. A properly written exam answer should tabulate iteration $k$, $U_2$, $U_3$, $U_4$, and $\Vert \Delta U \Vert_2$ across rows $k = 1, 2, 3, 4$. **Model Solution Outline for (b):** - CG converges in **at most $n$ steps** for an $n \times n$ SPD system in exact arithmetic; in practice, the number of iterations is bounded by $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{\kappa([K])})$, where $\kappa([K]) = \lambda_{max}/\lambda_{min}$. - Gauss–Seidel convergence is **linear** with rate $\rho$ depending on the spectral radius of the iteration matrix, often much slower. - In a full femur model with millions of DOFs, the condition number of $[K]$ is large (~$10^5$–$10^8$ depending on mesh aspect ratio), making CG the de-facto standard in solvers like ABAQUS and ANSYS. - CG also requires **only matrix-vector products**, allowing efficient use of sparse matrix storage (CSR, CSC). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing "add" with "assign":** Students often write $K_{22} = k^{(1)} + k^{(2)}$ which loses the conceptual point. The correct phrasing is $K_{22} \leftarrow K_{22} + k_{22}^{(2)}$ because $K_{22}$ already holds $k_{22}^{(1)}$. > 2. **Forgetting the sign in off-diagonals:** $K_{12} = K_{21} = -k^{(1)}$ — students frequently drop the negative sign and lose 1 mark. > 3. **Wrong IEN mapping:** Drawing element 1 as connecting nodes 1-3 instead of 1-2 silently corrupts the entire $[K]$. > 4. **Units in stress calculation:** Converting $E$ in GPa to Pa before multiplying by strain in 1/m. Many students write $\sigma = 110 \times \text{strain}$ and end up with a stress 9 orders of magnitude too small. > 5. **Skipping the symmetry check:** After assembly, always verify $[K] = [K]^T$. A non-symmetric $[K]$ means assembly is wrong. > 6. **Forgetting boundary conditions in iterative solver:** The penalty or row-elimination must be applied before iteration, not after. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Assembly Equation (Memorize):** $K_{IJ} = \sum_{e} k_{ij}^e$ where $I, J$ come from the IEN table. - **Element Stiffness (1D Bar):** $[k]^e = \dfrac{AE}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ — symmetric, singular, PSD. - **Global Stiffness Properties:** Square, symmetric, **positive semi-definite** (PSD) before BCs, **positive definite** (PD) after applying essential BCs, sparse, banded. - **Assembly is Addition, Not Replacement:** Always use $\leftarrow$ (additive update). - **Direct vs Iterative Choice Rule:** Use **direct** for $n < 5000$ DOFs; use **iterative (CG)** for $n \ge 5000$ DOFs. - **Cholesky is valid only if** $[K]$ is **symmetric positive definite** (always true for well-posed biomechanical stiffness). - **Convergence criterion for iterative solvers:** $\dfrac{\Vert U^{(k+1)} - U^{(k)} \Vert_2}{\Vert U^{(k+1)} \Vert_2} \le \varepsilon$, typically $\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$ for engineering accuracy. - **SOR parameter $\omega$:** Optimal $\omega \in (1, 2)$; $\omega = 1$ reduces to Gauss–Seidel, $\omega \ge 2$ diverges. - **CG convergence rate:** $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{\kappa([K])})$ — better mesh $\Rightarrow$ smaller $\kappa$ $\Rightarrow$ faster convergence. - **Post-processing step always follows the solution** — nodal $\{U\}$ alone is incomplete; element stresses and strains are the clinically/biomedically meaningful output. - **Biomedical relevance tags:** Orthopedic implants (stress), cardiovascular stents (radial expansion), dental biomechanics, hyperthermia (bioheat), soft tissue deformation, scoliosis bracing. - **Safety check habit:** Always compute $\sigma_{max}$ and compare to the **yield strength** of the biomaterial; report the **Factor of Safety (FoS) = yield/$\sigma_{max}$**. - **Common material values to memorize:** Cortical bone $E \approx 17$ GPa, Titanium $E \approx 110$ GPa, Stainless Steel 316L $E \approx 200$ GPa, Aortic wall $E \approx 0.5$ MPa (nonlinear).

topics.title: Finite Element Method for Nonlinear ODEs - Linearization and Newton-Raphson Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT634 - COMPUTATIONAL METHODS IN BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Finite Element Method for Nonlinear ODEs - Linearization and Newton-Raphson Method # Finite Element Method for Nonlinear ODEs — Linearization and Newton–Raphson Method ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **nonlinear ordinary differential equation (ODE)** in the context of the Finite Element Method (FEM) is any boundary value problem in which the unknown function $u(x)$ and/or its derivatives appear in a nonlinear fashion within the governing differential operator. After applying the standard Galerkin or weighted-residual finite element discretization, such an ODE is transformed into a system of **nonlinear algebraic equations** of the form: $$\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}) = \mathbf{0}$$ where $\mathbf{U} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the global vector of nodal unknowns and $\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U})$ is the global residual vector. Because this system cannot be solved directly by Gaussian elimination, it must be **linearized** at each iteration. The **Newton–Raphson Method** is the canonical iterative scheme used to compute successive corrections $\Delta\mathbf{U}$ by solving the linearized tangent system: $$\mathbf{K}_T(\mathbf{U}^{(k)})\,\Delta\mathbf{U}^{(k)} = -\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}^{(k)})$$ where $\mathbf{K}_T = \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{R}}{\partial \mathbf{U}}$ is the **Jacobian** or **tangent stiffness matrix** evaluated at the current iterate $\mathbf{U}^{(k)}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Newton–Raphson method is the **only** iterative linearization technique explicitly named in Module 4 of PEBMT634. Students must be able to (a) derive the tangent matrix for a given nonlinear ODE weak form, (b) write down the iteration update formula, and (c) state the convergence criteria. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Walking Downhill in a Fog" Imagine you are standing on a hillside in thick fog and want to reach the bottom (the **root** of your nonlinear equation). You cannot see the valley floor, so you cannot walk directly downhill. Each step, you feel the slope under your feet — this is like evaluating the **derivative** (the Jacobian) at your current position. You take one step in the direction the slope tells you is steepest downward, then stop, feel the slope again, and repeat. - Your **position** is $\mathbf{U}^{(k)}$. - The **height** is the residual $\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}^{(k)})$. - The **slope under your feet** is the tangent matrix $\mathbf{K}_T$. - Each **step** is a Newton correction $\Delta\mathbf{U}^{(k)}$. In a **finite element context**, the "hillside" is the entire finite element mesh — each nodal degree of freedom is a coordinate in a high-dimensional landscape, and the slope is computed element-by-element and assembled. > [!NOTE] > **Physical/Biomedical Constants Referenced Later:** > - **Young's modulus (E)** of soft tissue: order of **10 kPa** (e.g., brain ≈ 1–10 kPa, liver ≈ 10–20 kPa). > - **Strain energy density** of arteries: order of **10–100 kPa**. > - These appear in the nonlinear constitutive models that motivate this method. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 From Nonlinear ODE to Nonlinear Algebraic System ### 2.1.1 The Strong Form Consider a generic **second-order nonlinear ODE** on a one-dimensional domain $\Omega = [0, L]$: $$-\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(a(x, u)\frac{du}{dx}\right) + b(x, u)\,\frac{du}{dx} + c(x, u)\,u = f(x), \quad x \in \Omega$$ subject to boundary conditions, typically a mix of **Dirichlet** $u(0) = u_0$ and **Neumann** $a(L)\,u'(L) = g$. The coefficients $a, b, c$ may all depend on $u$ (or its derivatives), which makes the problem **nonlinear**. Common biomedical examples: - **Heat conduction in tissue with temperature-dependent conductivity**: $a = k_0(1 + \alpha T)$. - **Reaction–diffusion with Michaelis–Menten kinetics**: $c(u) = \dfrac{V_{\max}\,u}{K_m + u}$. - **Large-deflection beam/arterial wall mechanics**: $a(u)$ depends on stretch. ### 2.1.2 The Weak (Galerkin) Form Multiply by a test function $v(x)$ that vanishes on the Dirichlet boundary, integrate over $\Omega$, and integrate the highest-derivative term by parts: $$\int_0^L a(x, u)\,\frac{du}{dx}\,\frac{dv}{dx}\,dx + \int_0^L b(x, u)\,\frac{du}{dx}\,v\,dx + \int_0^L c(x, u)\,u\,v\,dx = \int_0^L f(x)\,v\,dx + g\,v(L)$$ The left-hand side is the **bilinear/trilinear form residual**; the right-hand side is the **load vector**. We denote the weak residual as: $$R(u; v) = \int_0^L \left[a\,u'v' + b\,u'v + c\,u\,v - f\,v\right] dx - g\,v(L) = 0$$ ### 2.1.3 FE Discretization Approximate $u(x) \approx u_h(x) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} N_j(x)\,U_j$, where $N_j(x)$ are piecewise-linear (or higher-order) **shape functions** and $U_j$ are **nodal unknowns**. Substituting $u_h$ into $R$ and choosing $v = N_i$ (Galerkin) yields, for $i = 1, \dots, n$: $$R_i(\mathbf{U}) = \sum_{e=1}^{n_{el}} \int_{\Omega_e} \left[a\,u_h'\,N_i' + b\,u_h'\,N_i + c\,u_h\,N_i - f\,N_i\right] dx - g\,N_i(L) = 0$$ This is a **system of $n$ coupled nonlinear algebraic equations** in the unknowns $\mathbf{U} = (U_1, U_2, \dots, U_n)^T$: $$\boxed{\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}) \equiv \mathbf{F}^{int}(\mathbf{U}) - \mathbf{F}^{ext} = \mathbf{0}}$$ where $\mathbf{F}^{int}$ is the **internal force vector** (arising from the differential operator) and $\mathbf{F}^{ext}$ is the **external load vector**. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Linearization" is needed:** A linear system $\mathbf{K}\mathbf{U} = \mathbf{F}$ can be inverted in one step. The nonlinear system has no closed-form solution; we must repeatedly solve **linear approximations** to it. ## 2.2 The Newton–Raphson Iteration ### 2.2.1 Derivation of the Linearized Tangent System Suppose we have an approximation $\mathbf{U}^{(k)}$ at iteration $k$. We seek a correction $\Delta\mathbf{U}^{(k)}$ such that $\mathbf{U}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{U}^{(k)} + \Delta\mathbf{U}^{(k)}$ makes the residual vanish. Perform a **first-order Taylor expansion** of $\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U})$ about $\mathbf{U}^{(k)}$: $$\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}^{(k)} + \Delta\mathbf{U}^{(k)}) \approx \mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}^{(k)}) + \underbrace{\left.\frac{\partial \mathbf{R}}{\partial \mathbf{U}}\right|_{\mathbf{U}^{(k)}}}_{\mathbf{K}_T(\mathbf{U}^{(k)})}\,\Delta\mathbf{U}^{(k)}$$ Setting this to zero gives the **Newton–Raphson linear system**: $$\mathbf{K}_T(\mathbf{U}^{(k)})\,\Delta\mathbf{U}^{(k)} = -\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}^{(k)})$$ The update rule is: $$\boxed{\mathbf{U}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{U}^{(k)} - \left[\mathbf{K}_T(\mathbf{U}^{(k)})\right]^{-1}\,\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}^{(k)})}$$ ### 2.2.2 Tangent (Jacobian) Matrix for a Single Element For element $e$ with nodes $i, j$ and shape functions $N_i, N_j$: $$K_T^{(e)}_{mn} = \frac{\partial R_m^{(e)}}{\partial U_n} = \int_{\Omega_e} \left[\frac{\partial a}{\partial u}\,u' N_m' N_n' + a\,N_m' N_n' + \frac{\partial b}{\partial u}\,u' N_m N_n + b\,N_m N_n + \frac{\partial c}{\partial u}\,u N_m N_n + c\,N_m N_n\right] dx$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** The tangent matrix contains the **original (linear) stiffness terms** plus **extra terms** $\dfrac{\partial a}{\partial u}, \dfrac{\partial b}{\partial u}, \dfrac{\partial c}{\partial u}$ multiplied by the current solution $u_h$. This is what makes it "tangent" — it depends on the **state**. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Units (Biomedical Context) | |---|----------|---------------------|----------------------------| | 1 | Strong form (1-D nonlinear BVP) | $-\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left(a(u)\,u'\right) + b(u)\,u' + c(u)\,u = f$ | — | | 2 | Weak residual | $R_i(\mathbf{U}) = \int_\Omega [a\,u_h' N_i' + b\,u_h' N_i + c\,u_h N_i - f N_i]\,dx - g N_i(L)$ | N (force) | | 3 | Global nonlinear system | $\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}) = \mathbf{F}^{int}(\mathbf{U}) - \mathbf{F}^{ext} = \mathbf{0}$ | N | | 4 | Newton linearization | $\mathbf{K}_T(\mathbf{U}^{(k)})\,\Delta\mathbf{U}^{(k)} = -\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}^{(k)})$ | — | | 5 | Update rule | $\mathbf{U}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{U}^{(k)} + \Delta\mathbf{U}^{(k)}$ | varies | | 6 | Tangent (Jacobian) matrix | $\mathbf{K}_T = \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{R}}{\partial \mathbf{U}}$ | N/m (stiffness) | | 7 | Convergence: residual norm | $\dfrac{\Vert \mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}^{(k)}) \Vert_2}{\Vert \mathbf{F}^{ext} \Vert_2} \leq \epsilon_R$ | dimensionless | | 8 | Convergence: increment norm | $\dfrac{\Vert \Delta\mathbf{U}^{(k)} \Vert_2}{\Vert \mathbf{U}^{(k)} \Vert_2} \leq \epsilon_U$ | dimensionless | | 9 | Typical tolerances | $\epsilon_R = 10^{-6},\ \epsilon_U = 10^{-8}$ | — | | 10 | Quadratic convergence rate | $\Vert e^{(k+1)} \Vert \leq C\,\Vert e^{(k)} \Vert^2$ | — | | 11 | Element stiffness (linear part) | $K_{mn}^{(e)} = \int_{\Omega_e} [a\,N_m' N_n' + b\,N_m N_n' + c\,N_m N_n]\,dx$ | N/m | | 12 | Geometric/material nonlinearity extra term | $K_{mn}^{NL,(e)} = \int_{\Omega_e} [\partial_u a\,u_h' N_m' N_n' + \partial_u b\,u_h' N_m N_n + \partial_u c\,u_h N_m N_n]\,dx$ | N/m | > [!WARNING] > **LaTeX Isolation Rule:** All standalone `$$` blocks above are surrounded by **one blank line** above and below. Inline math uses `$...$`. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Biomedical Engineering - **Arterial wall mechanics (large deformation):** The stress-stretch relation $\sigma = E(\lambda - 1/\lambda^2)$ (neo-Hookean) is nonlinear; FEM + Newton–Raphson simulates stent deployment. - **Tumor growth models:** Reaction–diffusion equation with proliferation rate $\gamma(u) = \gamma_0 \exp(-u/K)$ — nonlinearity solved iteratively. - **Drug-eluting stents:** Diffusion with concentration-dependent solubility. - **EEG/forward head models:** Poisson equation with heterogeneous (nonlinear in extreme cases) tissue conductivity. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Derivation 1 — Tangent Matrix of a Nonlinear Diffusion Equation **Problem:** Solve $-\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left(k(u)\,\dfrac{du}{dx}\right) = f(x)$ on $[0, L]$ with $u(0)=0,\ u'(L)=0$, where $k(u) = k_0(1 + \alpha u)$. ### Step 1: Write the Strong Form $$\mathcal{L}(u) \equiv -\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(k_0(1+\alpha u)\,u'\right) - f(x) = 0$$ ### Step 2: Multiply by Test Function $v$ and Integrate $$\int_0^L \left[-\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(k(u)\,u'\right) - f\right] v\,dx = 0$$ ### Step 3: Integrate the Diffusion Term by Parts Using $\displaystyle\int_0^L \frac{d}{dx}(\cdot)\,v\,dx = \left[(\cdot)\,v\right]_0^L - \int_0^L (\cdot)\,v'\,dx$, and noting $v(0)=0$ (Galerkin test) and the Neumann condition $k(L)u'(L)=0$: $$\int_0^L k(u)\,u'\,v'\,dx - \int_0^L f\,v\,dx = 0$$ ### Step 4: Substitute FE Approximation Let $u_h(x) = \sum_{j=1}^n N_j(x) U_j$, $v = N_i(x)$: $$R_i(\mathbf{U}) = \int_0^L k(u_h)\,\left(\sum_j N_j' U_j\right) N_i'\,dx - \int_0^L f\,N_i\,dx = 0$$ ### Step 5: Identify Internal Force and External Load $$\underbrace{\int_0^L k(u_h)\,\left(\sum_j N_j' U_j\right) N_i'\,dx}_{F_i^{int}(\mathbf{U})} = \underbrace{\int_0^L f\,N_i\,dx}_{F_i^{ext}}$$ ### Step 6: Differentiate $R_i$ with Respect to $U_n$ to Get Tangent Matrix Use the **product rule** on $k(u_h)\,u_h'\,N_i'$: $$\frac{\partial R_i}{\partial U_n} = \int_0^L \left[\frac{\partial k}{\partial u}\,N_n\,u_h'\,N_i' + k(u_h)\,N_n'\,N_i'\right] dx$$ ### Step 7: Substitute $k(u) = k_0(1+\alpha u)$ and $\partial k/\partial u = k_0\alpha$ $$\boxed{K_{T,in} = \int_0^L \left[k_0\alpha\,u_h'\,N_n'\,N_i' + k_0(1+\alpha u_h)\,N_n'\,N_i'\right] dx}$$ The first term is the **extra nonlinear contribution**; the second is the **linear part evaluated with the current $u_h$** (it depends on $\mathbf{U}^{(k)}$). ### Step 8: Single Linear Element on $[x_e, x_{e+1}]$ For a 2-node element of length $h_e$ with linear shape functions $N_1, N_2$: - $N_1' = -1/h_e,\ N_2' = 1/h_e$ - $u_h' = (U_2 - U_1)/h_e$ (constant over element) - $u_h = (U_1 + U_2)/2$ (average, but varies linearly; we use the midpoint) For a representative midpoint value $\bar{u}_e = (U_1^{(k)} + U_2^{(k)})/2$: $$K_T^{(e)} = \frac{k_0}{h_e}\begin{bmatrix} 1 + \alpha\bar{u}_e + \alpha\Delta U_e/2 & -(1+\alpha\bar{u}_e) \\ -(1+\alpha\bar{u}_e) & 1+\alpha\bar{u}_e - \alpha\Delta U_e/2 \end{bmatrix}$$ where $\Delta U_e = U_2^{(k)} - U_1^{(k)}$. This shows the **asymmetry introduced by the nonlinear conductivity**. ### Step 9: Newton Iteration At each step $k$: 1. Compute $\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}^{(k)})$. 2. Assemble and factor $\mathbf{K}_T(\mathbf{U}^{(k)})$. 3. Solve $\mathbf{K}_T\,\Delta\mathbf{U} = -\mathbf{R}$. 4. Update $\mathbf{U}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{U}^{(k)} + \Delta\mathbf{U}$. 5. Check convergence; if not satisfied, go to (1). --- ## 3.2 Detailed Derivation 2 — Newton–Raphson Convergence Rate Let $\mathbf{e}^{(k)} = \mathbf{U}^{(k)} - \mathbf{U}^*$ be the error at iteration $k$. The exact residual satisfies $\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}^*) = \mathbf{0}$. Taylor-expand $\mathbf{R}$ around $\mathbf{U}^*$: $$\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}^{(k)}) = \mathbf{K}_T(\mathbf{U}^*)\,\mathbf{e}^{(k)} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{U}^*)\,[\mathbf{e}^{(k)}]^2 + \mathcal{O}(\Vert\mathbf{e}\Vert^3)$$ Newton's correction gives $\Delta\mathbf{U}^{(k)} = -\mathbf{K}_T^{-1}\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}^{(k)})$. Therefore: $$\mathbf{e}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{e}^{(k)} - \mathbf{K}_T^{-1}\left[\mathbf{K}_T\,\mathbf{e}^{(k)} + \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{H}\,(\mathbf{e}^{(k)})^2 + \dots\right]$$ $$= -\tfrac{1}{2}\,\mathbf{K}_T^{-1}\,\mathbf{H}\,(\mathbf{e}^{(k)})^2 + \mathcal{O}(\Vert\mathbf{e}\Vert^3)$$ Taking norms: $$\boxed{\Vert\mathbf{e}^{(k+1)}\Vert \leq C\,\Vert\mathbf{e}^{(k)}\Vert^2,\quad C = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\Vert\mathbf{K}_T^{-1}\mathbf{H}\Vert}$$ This proves **quadratic convergence** — provided the initial guess is sufficiently close to the root. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Newton–Raphson vs Other Linearization Schemes | Feature | Newton–Raphson | Modified Newton | Picard (Fixed-Point) | Secant / Quasi-Newton (BFGS) | |---------|---------------|-----------------|---------------------|------------------------------| | Tangent matrix updated | **Every iteration** | Once (initial) | Not used | Approximated | | Convergence rate | **Quadratic** | Linear | Linear | Superlinear | | Cost per iteration | Highest (factor + solve) | Lowest | Moderate | Moderate | | Robustness to bad initial guess | Low (needs good $\mathbf{U}^{(0)}$) | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | | Implementation complexity | Moderate | Low | Low | High | | KTU 2024 syllabus coverage | **Yes (explicit)** | Mention only | Mention only | No | | Best biomedical use case | Large-deformation soft tissue | Simple constitutive models | Mild nonlinearity | Inverse problems, parameter ID | --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Compact) **Equation:** $-u'' + u^2 = 1$ on $[0,1]$ with $u(0) = u(1) = 0$. Discretize with 2 linear elements (3 nodes). **Step 1 — Local matrices for a generic element of length $h$:** $$K_L = \frac{1}{h}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1\end{bmatrix} + \frac{h}{6}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2\end{bmatrix}$$ The first matrix comes from the linear term $-u''$; the second from the Galerkin treatment of $u^2$ at the current iterate. Specifically, $u_h^2\,N_i$ contributes a **nonlinear load-like vector** at iteration $k$: $$F_i^{NL,(k)} = \int_{\Omega_e} (u_h^{(k)})^2\,N_i\,dx$$ So the residual is $\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}) = \mathbf{K}_L\mathbf{U} - \mathbf{F}^{NL}(\mathbf{U}) - \mathbf{F}^{ext}$, and the tangent is: $$\mathbf{K}_T = \mathbf{K}_L - \frac{\partial \mathbf{F}^{NL}}{\partial \mathbf{U}} = \mathbf{K}_L - 2\int_{\Omega_e} u_h\,N_n N_i\,dx$$ For $h=0.5$, this gives tractable $3\times3$ system. Two Newton iterations with $\mathbf{U}^{(0)} = \mathbf{0}$ typically yield a solution accurate to machine precision. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Newton–Raphson FEM Algorithm Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Problem Definition
Nonlinear ODE with BCs] --> B[Derive Weak Form
Multiply by test function v
Integrate by parts] B --> C[FE Discretization
Approximate u by Nj x Uj] C --> D[Assemble Global Residual
R of U equals zero] D --> E[Initialize U at k equals zero
Set tolerance eps and max iter] E --> F[Compute Residual
R of U at iteration k] F --> G{Norm check
Residual smaller than epsR} G -- Yes --> Z[Solution converged
Output U at k] G -- No --> H[Assemble Tangent Matrix
K_T equals dR by dU at U at k] H --> I[Apply Boundary Conditions
Modify K_T and R] I --> J[Solve Linear System
K_T times deltaU equals negative R] J --> K[Update Solution
U at k plus one equals U at k plus deltaU] K --> L{k plus one less than max iter} L -- Yes --> F L -- No --> M[WARNING: Non-convergence
Reduce step or refine mesh] M --> Z ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Architecture of a Single Newton Iteration ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[Input State] U0[Nodal vector U at iteration k] BC[Boundary condition data] MAT[Material parameters k0, alpha] end subgraph CORE[Newton Iteration Core] R1[Element loop over mesh] R2[Compute element residual
Integrate k of u times u prime times N prime] R3[Compute element tangent
Add dk by du contribution] R4[Assemble global R and K_T] end subgraph SOLVE[Linear Solve Stage] S1[Apply Dirichlet BCs
Eliminate rows and columns] S2[LU factorize K_T] S3[Forward and back substitute
to obtain deltaU] end subgraph OUTPUT[Output Update] O1[Compute U at k plus one] O2[Check norms
Residual and increment] O3{Converged?} end U0 --> R1 BC --> R1 MAT --> R2 R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 R4 --> S1 --> S2 --> S3 S3 --> O1 --> O2 --> O3 O3 -- Yes --> END[Return U at k plus one as final] O3 -- No --> LOOP[Loop back to Newton Iteration Core] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Convergence Behaviour Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Nonlinear ODE problem] --> Q{Choose Linearization Strategy} Q --> R1[Picard / Fixed-Point
Linear convergence] Q --> R2[Modified Newton
Constant K_T, Linear convergence] Q --> R3[Full Newton Raphson
Quadratic convergence] R1 --> S1[Many iterations needed
Robust to bad start] R2 --> S2[Cheap per step
Slower than full Newton] R3 --> S3[Few iterations near root
Needs good initial guess] S1 --> T[KTU recommendation for biomedical
problems with mild nonlinearity] S3 --> U[KTU preferred for stiff nonlinear
tissue mechanics problems] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4] **State the Newton–Raphson iteration formula for solving the nonlinear algebraic system $\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}) = \mathbf{0}$ that arises from the finite element discretization of a nonlinear ODE. Define each term.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Newton–Raphson iteration is: $$\mathbf{U}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{U}^{(k)} - \left[\mathbf{K}_T(\mathbf{U}^{(k)})\right]^{-1}\,\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}^{(k)})$$ where: - $\mathbf{U}^{(k)}$ is the nodal solution vector at iteration $k$ **(1 mark)**, - $\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}^{(k)})$ is the global residual vector evaluated at $\mathbf{U}^{(k)}$ **(1 mark)**, - $\mathbf{K}_T(\mathbf{U}^{(k)}) = \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{R}}{\partial \mathbf{U}}\bigg|_{\mathbf{U}^{(k)}}$ is the Jacobian / tangent stiffness matrix at $\mathbf{U}^{(k)}$ **(1 mark)**. Equivalently, solve the linear system $\mathbf{K}_T\,\Delta\mathbf{U}^{(k)} = -\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}^{(k)})$ and update $\mathbf{U}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{U}^{(k)} + \Delta\mathbf{U}^{(k)}$. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4] **List any two convergence criteria used to terminate the Newton–Raphson iteration in FEM, and write the corresponding mathematical expressions.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Criterion 1 — Residual norm (1.5 marks):** $$\dfrac{\Vert \mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}^{(k)}) \Vert_2}{\Vert \mathbf{F}^{ext} \Vert_2} \leq \epsilon_R,\quad \text{typical } \epsilon_R = 10^{-6}$$ **Criterion 2 — Increment norm (1.5 marks):** $$\dfrac{\Vert \Delta\mathbf{U}^{(k)} \Vert_2}{\Vert \mathbf{U}^{(k)} \Vert_2} \leq \epsilon_U,\quad \text{typical } \epsilon_U = 10^{-8}$$ (Alternative acceptable form: maximum component test — $\max_i \vert \Delta U_i^{(k)} \vert / \max_i \vert U_i^{(k)} \vert \leq \epsilon_U$.) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) ### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper Style]` **(a)** Starting from the strong form $-\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left(k(u)\,\dfrac{du}{dx}\right) = f(x)$ on $[0, L]$ with mixed boundary conditions, derive the **Galerkin weak form** and show how it leads to the nonlinear algebraic system $\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}) = \mathbf{0}$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For the nonlinear ODE $-u'' + u^3 = 1$ on $[0, 1]$ with $u(0) = u(1) = 0$, using two equal linear elements of length $h = 0.5$, derive the **tangent stiffness matrix** and write down the **first Newton–Raphson iteration** explicitly. **(7 marks)** ### Model Answer — Question A #### Part (a) — Derivation of Weak Form and Nonlinear System (7 marks) **Step 1 (1 mark):** Multiply the strong form by a test function $v(x)$ that satisfies $v(0)=0$: $$\int_0^L \left[-\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(k(u)\,u'\right) - f\right] v\,dx = 0$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Integration by parts on the second-order term (using $v(0)=0$ and the natural BC $k(L)u'(L) = g_L$): $$\int_0^L k(u)\,u'\,v'\,dx - \int_0^L f\,v\,dx = g_L\,v(L)$$ This is the **Galerkin weak form**. **Step 3 (1 mark):** Introduce FE approximation $u_h(x) = \sum_{j=1}^n N_j(x) U_j$ and choose $v = N_i(x)$: $$\int_0^L k(u_h)\,\left(\sum_j N_j' U_j\right) N_i'\,dx = \int_0^L f\,N_i\,dx + g_L\,N_i(L)$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Define the residual component: $$R_i(\mathbf{U}) \equiv \int_0^L k(u_h)\,u_h'\,N_i'\,dx - \int_0^L f\,N_i\,dx - g_L\,N_i(L) = 0$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Recognize that because $k(u_h)$ depends on $u_h$ and hence on $\mathbf{U}$, the integrand is **nonlinear in $\mathbf{U}$**. Write in compact form: $$\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}) \equiv \mathbf{F}^{int}(\mathbf{U}) - \mathbf{F}^{ext} = \mathbf{0}$$ **Step 6 (1 mark):** Argue that this nonlinear system cannot be inverted in closed form; an **iterative linearization** is required. **Step 7 (1 mark):** Conclude that the tangent matrix is: $$K_{T,ij} = \frac{\partial R_i}{\partial U_j} = \int_0^L \left[\frac{\partial k}{\partial u}\,u_h'\,N_j'\,N_i' + k(u_h)\,N_j'\,N_i'\right]dx$$ --- #### Part (b) — Tangent Matrix and First Newton Iteration for $-u''+u^3=1$ (7 marks) **Step 1 (1 mark):** Weak form (with $k(u) = 1$ for the diffusion, and the cubic is a reaction term): $$\int_0^1 u'\,v'\,dx + \int_0^1 u_h^3\,v\,dx = \int_0^1 v\,dx$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Residual for node $i$: $$R_i(\mathbf{U}) = \int_0^1 u_h'\,N_i'\,dx + \int_0^1 u_h^3\,N_i\,dx - \int_0^1 N_i\,dx$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Differentiate to get tangent: $$K_{T,ij} = \frac{\partial R_i}{\partial U_j} = \underbrace{\int_0^1 N_j'\,N_i'\,dx}_{K_L} + \underbrace{3\int_0^1 u_h^2\,N_j\,N_i\,dx}_{K_{NL}}$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** For $h=0.5$ and linear shape functions, the **linear part** of each element is: $$K_L^{(e)} = \frac{1}{0.5}\begin{bmatrix}1 & -1\\ -1 & 1\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}2 & -2\\ -2 & 2\end{bmatrix}$$ Assembled (3 nodes, 2 elements) global linear stiffness: $$K_L = \begin{bmatrix}2 & -2 & 0\\ -2 & 4 & -2\\ 0 & -2 & 2\end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** With initial guess $\mathbf{U}^{(0)} = (0, 0, 0)^T$, we have $u_h^2 = 0$, so $K_{NL}^{(0)} = 0$. Hence: $$\mathbf{K}_T^{(0)} = K_L$$ **Step 6 (1 mark):** The internal force vector at $\mathbf{U}^{(0)} = 0$ is also zero (since $u_h^3 = 0$). Residual: $$\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}^{(0)}) = -\mathbf{F}^{ext},\quad F_i^{ext} = \int_0^1 N_i\,dx$$ With $F_1^{ext} = F_3^{ext} = h/2 = 0.25$ and $F_2^{ext} = h = 0.5$: $$\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}^{(0)}) = (-0.25, -0.5, -0.25)^T$$ **Step 7 (1 mark):** Solve $K_L\,\Delta\mathbf{U}^{(0)} = -\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}^{(0)}) = (0.25, 0.5, 0.25)^T$. By symmetry and known pattern, $\Delta\mathbf{U}^{(0)} = (0.0625, 0.125, 0.0625)^T$. The first Newton iterate is: $$\mathbf{U}^{(1)} = (0.0625,\ 0.125,\ 0.0625)^T$$ (The exact solution of $-u''+u^3=1$ has $u(0.5) \approx 0.4023$, so further iterations are needed — and they will converge quadratically.) --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Model Paper Style]` **(a)** Explain the physical/biomedical motivation for using the Newton–Raphson method in finite element analysis of nonlinear ODEs. Give **two specific biomedical examples** and for each, identify the source of nonlinearity. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a nonlinear boundary value problem discretized by FEM, prove that the Newton–Raphson iteration exhibits **quadratic convergence** in a neighbourhood of the exact solution. State the conditions required for this convergence. **(7 marks)** ### Model Answer — Question B #### Part (a) — Biomedical Motivation and Examples (7 marks) **Introduction (2 marks):** Real biological tissues and physiological processes frequently exhibit nonlinear constitutive behaviour — the relationship between cause and effect is not proportional. When such a system is modeled by an ODE/BVP and discretized via FEM, the resulting algebraic system is nonlinear. The Newton–Raphson method is the canonical iterative linearization because it converges in very few iterations near the solution, which is essential for clinical/biomedical computational workflows. **Example 1 — Large-deformation mechanics of arterial wall (2.5 marks):** The neo-Hookean hyperelastic model for an artery under internal blood pressure $p$ gives stress $\sigma = E(\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$, where $\lambda = 1 + u/R_0$ is the stretch ratio and $u$ is the radial displacement. The governing ODE is: $$-\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(\sigma(r)\,\frac{du}{dr}\right) + \frac{\sigma(r)}{r} = p$$ Nonlinearity source: $\sigma$ depends on $u$ through $\lambda$ — material nonlinearity (2 marks for the example). **Example 2 — Reaction–diffusion in tumor growth (2.5 marks):** The nutrient-limited tumor proliferation equation: $$-D\,\nabla^2 c + \frac{V_{\max}\,c}{K_m + c} = 0$$ Nonlinearity source: the Michaelis–Menten uptake term $\dfrac{V_{\max}\,c}{K_m + c}$ depends nonlinearly on concentration $c$. (2 marks for the example.) (Other acceptable examples: nonlinear drug-elution kinetics, EEG forward problem with anisotropic tissue conductivity, nonlinear heat generation in hyperthermia treatment.) --- #### Part (b) — Proof of Quadratic Convergence (7 marks) **Setup (2 marks):** Let $\mathbf{U}^*$ be the exact root so that $\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}^*) = \mathbf{0}$. Assume $\mathbf{R}$ is twice continuously differentiable in a neighbourhood $\mathcal{N}$ of $\mathbf{U}^*$, and that the Jacobian $\mathbf{K}_T(\mathbf{U}^*) = \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{R}}{\partial \mathbf{U}}\big|_{\mathbf{U}^*}$ is **nonsingular**. **Taylor expansion (2 marks):** For $\mathbf{U}^{(k)} \in \mathcal{N}$, expand $\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}^{(k)})$ about $\mathbf{U}^*$: $$\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}^{(k)}) = \mathbf{K}_T(\mathbf{U}^*)\,\mathbf{e}^{(k)} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mathbf{H}(\xi)\,[\mathbf{e}^{(k)},\mathbf{e}^{(k)}]$$ where $\mathbf{e}^{(k)} = \mathbf{U}^{(k)} - \mathbf{U}^*$ and $\mathbf{H}$ is the second-derivative tensor. **Newton step analysis (2 marks):** The Newton correction is: $$\mathbf{e}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{U}^{(k)} - \mathbf{K}_T^{-1}(\mathbf{U}^{(k)})\,\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}^{(k)}) - \mathbf{U}^*$$ Expanding $\mathbf{K}_T^{-1}(\mathbf{U}^{(k)})$ about $\mathbf{U}^*$ and using $\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}^{(k)}) = \mathbf{K}_T(\mathbf{U}^*)\mathbf{e}^{(k)} + \mathcal{O}(\Vert\mathbf{e}\Vert^2)$: $$\mathbf{e}^{(k+1)} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\,\mathbf{K}_T^{-1}(\mathbf{U}^*)\,\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{U}^*)\,[\mathbf{e}^{(k)}]^2 + \mathcal{O}(\Vert\mathbf{e}\Vert^3)$$ **Conclusion (1 mark):** Taking norms: $$\Vert\mathbf{e}^{(k+1)}\Vert \leq C\,\Vert\mathbf{e}^{(k)}\Vert^2,\qquad C = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\Vert\mathbf{K}_T^{-1}(\mathbf{U}^*)\,\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{U}^*)\Vert$$ This is **quadratic convergence**. **Required conditions (paid in the 7-mark distribution):** (i) $\mathbf{R} \in C^2$ near $\mathbf{U}^*$, (ii) $\mathbf{K}_T(\mathbf{U}^*)$ nonsingular, (iii) initial guess $\mathbf{U}^{(0)}$ sufficiently close to $\mathbf{U}^*$. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the derivative of the coefficient:** Students often write the tangent matrix as just the linear part $\int a N_i' N_j' dx$ and miss the $\partial a/\partial u$ contribution. This **loses 2–3 marks** every time. > 2. **Confusing iteration index with element index:** $k$ in $\mathbf{U}^{(k)}$ is the iteration number; $e$ in $\Omega_e$ is the element number. Do not interchange. > 3. **Sign error in the update:** The linear system is $\mathbf{K}_T\,\Delta\mathbf{U} = -\mathbf{R}$. A common mistake is $\mathbf{K}_T\,\Delta\mathbf{U} = +\mathbf{R}$. This yields divergence — and **3 marks deducted**. > 4. **Missing units in biomedical examples:** When asked for biomedical motivation, mention typical **numerical magnitudes** (e.g., E ≈ 10 kPa for soft tissue) to demonstrate engineering awareness — **partial marks awarded**. > 5. **No convergence criterion stated:** Even in derivations, briefly mention what stops the iteration — KTU examiners reward this. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] A nonlinear ODE, after FEM discretization, yields a **system of nonlinear algebraic equations** $\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}) = \mathbf{F}^{int}(\mathbf{U}) - \mathbf{F}^{ext} = \mathbf{0}$. - [x] The **Newton–Raphson** method linearizes $\mathbf{R}$ via a first-order Taylor expansion around the current iterate. - [x] The **tangent matrix** (Jacobian) is $\mathbf{K}_T(\mathbf{U}^{(k)}) = \partial \mathbf{R}/\partial \mathbf{U}\big|_{\mathbf{U}^{(k)}}$ — it depends on the current state. - [x] For a nonlinear diffusion coefficient $k(u)$, the tangent has **two terms**: $k(u)\,N_i' N_j'$ (linear-like) and $\partial_u k \cdot u'\,N_i' N_j'$ (extra). - [x] Iteration: solve $\mathbf{K}_T\,\Delta\mathbf{U}^{(k)} = -\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{U}^{(k)})$, then $\mathbf{U}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{U}^{(k)} + \Delta\mathbf{U}^{(k)}$. - [x] **Quadratic convergence** holds near the root; requires smooth $\mathbf{R}$ and nonsingular $\mathbf{K}_T(\mathbf{U}^*)$. - [x] Two practical **stopping criteria**: residual norm $\le \epsilon_R$ (typical $10^{-6}$) and increment norm $\le \epsilon_U$ (typical $10^{-8}$). - [x] **Biomedical applications**: arterial wall mechanics (neo-Hookean), tumor reaction-diffusion (Michaelis-Menten), drug-elution kinetics, EEG forward modeling. - [x] The **modified Newton** uses a constant tangent (cheaper, linear convergence); **Picard** iteration is another linear alternative but slower. - [x] A **good initial guess** $\mathbf{U}^{(0)}$ is essential — KTU problems often suggest using the solution of the linearized problem as the initial guess. - [x] The convergence proof is a standard Taylor-expansion argument — be ready to reproduce it in the exam. ---
